title: 10.1 Software Maintenance Overview
excerpt: Topic 10 — 10.1 Software Maintenance Overview 10.1 Software Maintenance Overview ← Topic 10 Software maintenance is the phase of the software life cycle that begins after delivery and continues for as long as the system remains in use. It encompasses all activities required to keep software operational, correct, and aligned with evolving user and environmental needs — see §2.1 SDLC for the mainten
full_text: Topic 10 — 10.1 Software Maintenance Overview 10.1 Software Maintenance Overview ← Topic 10 Software maintenance is the phase of the software life cycle that begins after delivery and continues for as long as the system remains in use. It encompasses all activities required to keep software operational, correct, and aligned with evolving user and environmental needs — see §2.1 SDLC for the maintenance phase within the overall life cycle. Definitions IEEE Std 1219 (1993): Software maintenance is the modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, to improve performance or other attributes, or to adapt the product to a modified environment. ISO/IEC 12207: Maintenance comprises the activities that occur when software code or associated documentation is modified due to a problem or because of the need for improvement or adaptation. Maintenance is a continuous process throughout the software life cycle — not merely a post-delivery activity that begins only after deployment. Every change during maintenance must follow formal change control — see §9.5 Change Control Process. Key aspects of maintenance Bug fixing (corrective maintenance): Identifying and correcting defects discovered in production use — see §10.4 Maintenance Categories. Enhancements (perfective maintenance): Adding new features or improving existing functionality to meet evolving user requirements. Performance optimization: Tuning the system to improve response time, throughput, or resource consumption. Porting and migration (adaptive maintenance): Adapting the software to new hardware platforms, operating systems, or network environments. Re-engineering: Restructuring legacy systems to improve maintainability without changing external behaviour — see §10.6 Software Re-engineering. Documentation: Updating user manuals, design documents, and inline comments so they reflect the current system state. Maintenance in the SDLC The SDLC explicitly includes a maintenance phase after deployment — maintenance consumes the majority of total life cycle effort and cost. Maintenance activities reuse the same engineering disciplines as development: analysis, design, implementation, and testing. Effective maintenance depends on configuration management, version control, and regression testing established during development — see Topic 9 — Processes & Configuration Management. Planned vs unplanned maintenance Planned maintenance: Scheduled activities such as periodic reviews, preventive refactoring, documentation updates, and scheduled upgrades. Unplanned maintenance: Reactive work triggered by unexpected failures, critical bugs, or urgent regulatory changes. Proactive maintenance: Anticipating future problems through code reviews, refactoring, and preventive measures before failures occur. Reactive maintenance: Responding to problems only after they have been reported or have caused service disruption. A mature maintenance organisation balances proactive and reactive work — over-reliance on reactive maintenance increases cost and risk. Maintenance stakeholders Original development team: Maintains the system they built; has the deepest knowledge but may move to new projects after delivery. In-house maintenance team: A dedicated group within the organisation that takes over after the development team hands off the product. Third-party maintenance: External vendors or contractors maintain the system under a support contract — common when the original developers are unavailable. Knowledge transfer from developers to maintainers is critical — poor handover leads to longer diagnosis times and higher maintenance cost. Maintenance process phases (ISO/IEC 12207) The ISO maintenance process defines seven sequential phases, each with defined inputs, activities, and outputs. PhaseInputsProcess / ActivitiesOutputs 1. Problem Identification User reports, monitoring alerts, change requests, audit findings Log and classify maintenance requests; assign priority; determine if change is needed Maintenance request record; problem classification 2. Analysis Maintenance request; current system documentation; source code Investigate root cause; perform impact analysis; estimate effort and cost; propose solution Analysis report; impact assessment; feasibility decision 3. Design Approved analysis report; existing design documents Design the modification; update affected modules; plan test cases; obtain design review approval Updated design specification; test plan 4. Implementation Approved design; source code baseline; change control authorisation Modify code and documentation; conduct code review; check in changes via SCM Modified software; updated documentation; change log entry 5. System Test Modified software; test plan; test environment Execute unit, integration, and system tests; perform regression testing; verify fix or enhancement Test results; defect reports; test completion report 6. Acceptance Test Tested software; acceptance criteria; user involvement User or customer validates that the change meets requirements; sign-off obtained Acceptance test report; user approval 7. Delivery Accepted software; release notes; deployment plan Deploy to production; update configuration baselines; notify stakeholders; close maintenance request Released software; updated baselines; maintenance closure record Maintenance request flow maintenance_request_flow.pseudo // ISO 12207 maintenance process — simplified flow REQUEST = receive_maintenance_request(user_report OR change_request) LOG request in maintenance tracking system CLASSIFY request AS corrective | adaptive | perfective | preventive ASSIGN priority (critical | high | medium | low) IF priority == critical THEN escalate to emergency patch process ELSE submit to change control board // see §9.5 END IF ANALYSIS = perform_impact_analysis(REQUEST, current_baseline) IF ANALYSIS.feasible == false THEN reject request WITH reason GOTO close_request END IF DESIGN modification based on ANALYSIS IMPLEMENT changes under SCM version control TEST modified system (unit → integration → system → regression) ACCEPTANCE_TEST with user/customer sign-off DELIVER to production; update baselines; close request Exam question — Software Maintenance Overview Q: Define software maintenance (IEEE and ISO). List key maintenance aspects. Describe the ISO maintenance process phases. Differentiate planned vs unplanned and proactive vs reactive maintenance. Model answer points: IEEE: modification after delivery to correct faults, improve performance/attributes, adapt to modified environment. ISO/IEC 12207: activities when code/docs modified due to problem or improvement/adaptation need. Continuous throughout lifecycle; not just post-delivery; part of SDLC maintenance phase. Key aspects: bug fixing, enhancements, performance, porting/migration, re-engineering, documentation. Seven phases: Problem Identification → Analysis → Design → Implementation → System Test → Acceptance Test → Delivery. Planned = scheduled; unplanned = reactive to failures; proactive = preventive; reactive = fix after failure. Stakeholders: original dev team, in-house maintenance, third-party; all changes via change control (§9.5). ← Topic 10 10.2 Software as Evolutionary Entity →

title: 10.2 Software as Evolutionary Entity
headings: Software as a model of the real world
Lehman's eight laws of software evolution
excerpt: Topic 10 — 10.2 Software as Evolutionary Entity 10.2 Software as Evolutionary Entity ← Topic 10 Software is not a static artifact — it is a dynamic model of the real world that must evolve as the world itself changes. This evolutionary nature is the fundamental reason maintenance exists and why it dominates the software life cycle — see §10.3 Need for Maintenance and §10.4 Maintenance Categories.
full_text: Topic 10 — 10.2 Software as Evolutionary Entity 10.2 Software as Evolutionary Entity ← Topic 10 Software is not a static artifact — it is a dynamic model of the real world that must evolve as the world itself changes. This evolutionary nature is the fundamental reason maintenance exists and why it dominates the software life cycle — see §10.3 Need for Maintenance and §10.4 Maintenance Categories. Software as a model of the real world Software encodes a simplified representation of business processes, physical systems, or user workflows. When the real world changes — new regulations, market conditions, technology, or user expectations — the software model must be updated to remain valid. A system that is never modified eventually becomes obsolete or incorrect relative to its operating environment. Maintenance is therefore not an optional add-on but an inherent consequence of software's role as a world model. E-type vs S-type software S-type (Static-type) software: Software that implements a well-understood, fixed specification — e.g. scientific computations with stable mathematical foundations; changes are rare and predictable. E-type (Evolutionary-type) software: Software that implements a real-world application and must evolve as the application domain changes — virtually all business and information systems are E-type. E-type software is subject to Lehman's laws of software evolution; S-type software is largely exempt from them. Most exam questions on software evolution refer to E-type systems. Lehman's eight laws of software evolution Meir Lehman (1980) formulated eight empirically observed laws governing E-type software evolution. Continuing Change: An E-type system must be continually adapted or it becomes progressively less useful over time. Increasing Complexity: As a system evolves, its structure becomes more complex unless active work is done to reduce complexity. Self Regulation: The evolution process is self-regulating — distribution of product and process measures tends to be near-normal. Conservation of Organisational Stability: The average effective global activity rate in an evolving E-type system is invariant over the product lifetime. Conservation of Familiarity: During active evolution, the content of successive releases is statistically invariant — roughly the same proportion of the system is changed in each release. Continuing Growth: The functional content of an E-type system must be continually increased to maintain user satisfaction over its lifetime. Declining Quality: The quality of an E-type system will appear to be declining unless it is rigorously maintained and adapted to operational environment changes. Feedback System: E-type systems constitute multi-loop, multi-agent feedback systems and must be treated as such to achieve significant improvement. Software is never "finished" Unlike manufactured products with a defined end-of-life, software systems can remain in active use for decades. Typical development takes 1–2 years, while maintenance may continue for 15–20 years or longer. More than 65% of total life cycle cost is spent on maintenance — see §10.5 Cost of Maintenance. Organisations that treat delivery as "project completion" underestimate the long-term commitment required. Legacy systems A legacy system is an older software system that remains in operational use despite being built with outdated technology, languages, or architectures. Legacy systems often lack adequate documentation, have high maintenance cost, and resist integration with modern platforms. Organisations face a strategic choice: continue maintaining, re-engineer, replace, or retire legacy systems — see §10.6 Re-engineering. Reverse engineering is often the first step in understanding a legacy system before deciding on a migration strategy — see §10.7 Reverse Engineering. Many critical business systems (banking, government, healthcare) are legacy systems that cannot be simply discarded. Exam question — Software as Evolutionary Entity Q: Explain why software is an evolutionary entity. State Lehman's eight laws. Differentiate E-type and S-type software. What is a legacy system? Model answer points: Software models the real world; real-world changes require software changes; maintenance is inherent not optional. E-type = real-world application, evolves with domain; S-type = fixed specification, stable, rare changes. Lehman's 8 laws: Continuing Change, Increasing Complexity, Self Regulation, Conservation of Organisational Stability, Conservation of Familiarity, Continuing Growth, Declining Quality, Feedback System. Software never finished: build 1–2 years, maintain 15–20 years; >65% of lifecycle cost in maintenance. Legacy system = old operational software with outdated tech, poor docs, high cost; options: maintain, re-engineer, replace, retire. ← 10.1 Software Maintenance Overview 10.3 Need for Maintenance →

title: 10.3 Need for Maintenance
headings: Importance of planning and supporting processes
excerpt: Topic 10 — 10.3 Need for Maintenance 10.3 Need for Maintenance ← Topic 10 Maintenance is required whenever software must remain correct, useful, and compatible with its operating environment. The reasons span technical defects, evolving requirements, and environmental change — each maps to one of the four maintenance categories described in §10.4 Maintenance Categories. Primary reasons for mainten
full_text: Topic 10 — 10.3 Need for Maintenance 10.3 Need for Maintenance ← Topic 10 Maintenance is required whenever software must remain correct, useful, and compatible with its operating environment. The reasons span technical defects, evolving requirements, and environmental change — each maps to one of the four maintenance categories described in §10.4 Maintenance Categories. Primary reasons for maintenance Correct faults: Fix bugs and errors discovered during production use that were not caught during testing. Improve design: Restructure internal modules to reduce complexity and improve future changeability without altering external behaviour. Implement enhancements: Add new features or extend existing functionality to meet evolving business needs. Interface with other systems: Modify the software to interoperate with newly introduced external systems, APIs, or data formats. Environmental and organisational drivers Accommodate new hardware/software/telecom: Adapt to new processors, operating systems, databases, network protocols, or cloud platforms. Migrate legacy systems: Move data and functionality from obsolete platforms to modern infrastructure while preserving business logic. Retire software: Plan and execute graceful decommissioning, data archival, and user migration to replacement systems. User requirement changes: Users discover new needs after deployment that were not captured in the original requirements specification. Regulatory and compliance changes: New laws or industry standards may require software modifications to remain compliant. Challenges of software maintenance Long lifespan (10–15 years): Systems outlive the developers who built them, creating knowledge gaps. Undocumented changes: Ad-hoc fixes applied without updating design documents accumulate technical debt over time. Legacy code: Old code written in obsolete languages with poor structure resists modification and testing. Complexity growth: Lehman's law of increasing complexity means each change makes the next change harder — see §10.2. Changing requirements: Users continuously request new features, making it difficult to stabilise the system. Interoperability: Integrating with new external systems requires understanding both the legacy system and the new interface standards. Lack of test coverage: Legacy systems often have no automated regression test suite, making every change risky. Skill gaps: Maintainers may lack expertise in the original programming language, framework, or domain. High cost: Maintenance consumes the majority of life cycle budget — see §10.5 Cost of Maintenance. Importance of planning and supporting processes A formal maintenance plan should be prepared before delivery, specifying staffing, procedures, SLAs, and escalation paths. Software Configuration Management (SCM) ensures every change is tracked, baselined, and reversible — see Topic 9 §9.1 SCM Overview. Change control prevents unauthorised modifications and ensures impact analysis before implementation — see §9.5 Change Control Process. Testing and regression testing verify that fixes and enhancements do not introduce new defects — see Topic 7 — Software Testing & Debugging. Documentation must be kept current so maintainers can understand system behaviour without relying on original developers. Design for maintainability during development — modular architecture, clear interfaces, and coding standards reduce future maintenance cost. Exam question — Need for Maintenance Q: List reasons why software maintenance is needed. What challenges make maintenance difficult? How do SCM, change control, and testing support maintenance? Model answer points: Reasons: correct faults, improve design, enhancements, interface with other systems, new hardware/OS, migrate legacy, retire software, user requirement changes. Challenges: 10–15 year lifespan, undocumented changes, legacy code, complexity growth, changing requirements, interoperability, lack of tests, skill gaps, high cost. Support: maintenance plan before delivery; SCM for tracking/baselines (Topic 9); change control for authorised changes (§9.5); regression testing (Topic 7); current documentation. ← 10.2 Software as Evolutionary Entity 10.4 Maintenance Categories →

title: 10.4 Maintenance Categories
headings: UGC NET 2016 — match maintenance type to description
excerpt: Topic 10 — 10.4 Maintenance Categories 10.4 Maintenance Categories ← Topic 10 IEEE and ISO/IEC 12207 classify all maintenance activities into four categories based on the purpose of the change — not based on who requested it or how urgent it is. Correct classification drives prioritisation, effort estimation, budget allocation, and change control routing — see §9.5 Change Control Process. Misclass
full_text: Topic 10 — 10.4 Maintenance Categories 10.4 Maintenance Categories ← Topic 10 IEEE and ISO/IEC 12207 classify all maintenance activities into four categories based on the purpose of the change — not based on who requested it or how urgent it is. Correct classification drives prioritisation, effort estimation, budget allocation, and change control routing — see §9.5 Change Control Process. Misclassification is a common exam trap — e.g. porting to a new OS is adaptive, not perfective. Formal definitions (IEEE / ISO) IEEE 1993: Software maintenance is modification of a software product after delivery to correct faults, improve performance or other attributes, or adapt the product to a modified environment. ISO/IEC 12207: Maintenance activities occur when software undergoes modifications to code and associated documentation due to a problem or a need for improvement or adaptation. All four categories fall under this umbrella — the category is determined by why the change is being made. After receiving a maintenance request, the first step is to identify the category; sometimes the reported problem is with the user, not the software — see §10.1 Overview. Four categories of maintenance CategoryFormal purposeTriggerExamples Corrective Correct discovered problems after delivery — faults that cause incorrect behaviour Bug report, failure in production, audit finding, security vulnerability Fixing a payroll calculation error; correcting a null-pointer crash; patching an SQL injection flaw; fixing incorrect tax computation in reports Adaptive Keep software usable in a changed or changing environment — functionality unchanged New OS, hardware, DBMS, regulations, business rules, or external system interfaces Porting from Windows Server 2016 to 2022; updating JDBC drivers for PostgreSQL 16; modifying code for new GDPR rules; interfacing with a new payment gateway API Perfective Improve performance, maintainability, or user-visible functionality after delivery User enhancement request, performance bottleneck, usability improvement, restructuring for changeability Adding export-to-PDF feature; reducing search response time from 5s to 0.5s; redesigning modules for easier modification; improving dashboard UI layout Preventive Detect and correct latent faults before they become effective faults; reduce future maintenance difficulty Proactive quality initiative, technical debt reduction, scheduled review Refactoring high-complexity modules; updating outdated design documents; adding automated backups; improving test coverage from 40% to 80%; adding monitoring and alerting Corrective maintenance — detail Corrective maintenance is triggered when the system behaves incorrectly — output is wrong, the system crashes, or a security flaw is discovered. It may be essential either to rectify bugs observed while the system is in use, or in some cases to enhance performance where the current behaviour is technically incorrect (e.g. slow response that violates the SLA). Corrective changes follow the full change control process: problem report → analysis → fix → test → deploy — though critical fixes may use an expedited path. Retesting verifies that the specific defect fix works; regression testing ensures the fix did not break existing functionality — both are mandatory after corrective changes. Corrective maintenance is typically the least expensive category per individual change, because the scope is narrow and the problem is well-defined. Adaptive maintenance — detail Adaptive maintenance modifies the product so it continues to work when the environment changes — the specified functional requirements remain the same. Common triggers include new operating systems, new hardware platforms, new database versions, changed government policies, revised business rules, and new external system interfaces. Adaptive work is often underestimated — porting may require changes throughout the codebase even when user-visible features are unchanged. Deferred adaptive maintenance leaves the system on unsupported platforms with security vulnerabilities and compliance risks. Example: a banking system must adapt when the central bank changes transaction reporting format — functionality is the same (report transactions) but the format changes. Perfective maintenance — detail Perfective maintenance is driven by user demands for new features or improvements to existing functionality — it is the largest category by effort. It includes improving functionality, performance, reliability, and restructuring the system to improve changeability (maintainability). Perfective changes expand scope compared to corrective fixes — they require requirements analysis, design updates, new test cases, and user documentation revisions. Because perfective requests are open-ended, they need strong change control and prioritisation to prevent scope creep — see §9.5. Example: users request a mobile-responsive version of an existing web dashboard — this is perfective (new capability), not adaptive (environment change). Preventive maintenance — detail Preventive maintenance addresses problems that are not yet critical but may cause serious failures if ignored — it reduces future corrective effort. It includes code optimisation, documentation updates, scheduled reviews, test suite expansion, backup procedures, and refactoring to reduce complexity. Preventive work is closely related to proactive maintenance — taking action before failures occur rather than reacting after them. In ISO terminology, preventive maintenance detects and corrects latent faults — defects present in the code that have not yet caused a visible failure. Preventive maintenance reduces the C (complexity) term in the maintenance effort formula — see §10.5 Cost of Maintenance. Proactive vs reactive maintenance DimensionProactiveReactive TimingScheduled before problems occurTriggered after a failure or complaint Primary categoryPreventive (and planned perfective/adaptive)Corrective (and emergency adaptive) ExamplesScheduled refactoring, documentation review, backup testingHotfix for production crash, emergency OS port after vendor EOL Cost patternLower long-term cost; predictable budgetHigher long-term cost; unpredictable spikes Planned maintenance activities (scheduled updates, backups, reviews) are proactive; unplanned activities triggered by crashes or security breaches are reactive. Organisations with strong preventive programmes spend less on emergency corrective work over the system lifetime. Corrective maintenance and patching Patching is emergency corrective maintenance — a fix applied under management pressure when a critical production defect cannot wait for the next scheduled release. Patches are deployed quickly but carry higher risk because impact analysis, full regression testing, and documentation updates may be abbreviated. Poorly managed patches often introduce unforeseen future errors — a patch in one module breaks another module that was not retested. Even emergency patches must not bypass change control entirely — at minimum: unique change number, impact analysis, retest of affected areas, and post-deployment monitoring. Every patch should be merged back into the main codebase through version control — see §9.6 Version Control — to prevent patch divergence. Typical effort and cost distribution Industry studies consistently show that maintenance effort is dominated by perfective work, not bug fixing — this is why maintenance budgets are often underestimated. Approximate distribution: ~60% perfective, ~20% adaptive, ~20% corrective. Preventive effort is often embedded within the other categories rather than tracked separately — e.g. refactoring done during a perfective change. Corrective maintenance is typically less expensive per change than perfective maintenance because scope is narrower and requirements are clearer. Organisations that budget maintenance only for bug fixes allocate roughly one-third of what they actually need. Category id

title: 10.5 Cost of Maintenance
headings: Why maintenance cost exceeds development cost
Components of maintenance cost and effort
Development vs maintenance — key differences driving cost
Risks and intangible costs of postponing maintenance
excerpt: Topic 10 — 10.5 Cost of Maintenance 10.5 Cost of Maintenance ← Topic 10 Software maintenance is the most expensive phase of the software life cycle — more than 65% of total cost is spent here, not during initial development. Understanding what drives maintenance cost, how to estimate effort using quantitative models, and how to reduce cost is essential for both project planning and exams. Maintena
full_text: Topic 10 — 10.5 Cost of Maintenance 10.5 Cost of Maintenance ← Topic 10 Software maintenance is the most expensive phase of the software life cycle — more than 65% of total cost is spent here, not during initial development. Understanding what drives maintenance cost, how to estimate effort using quantitative models, and how to reduce cost is essential for both project planning and exams. Maintenance category mix directly affects cost — see §10.4 Maintenance Categories. Why maintenance cost exceeds development cost More than 65% of total software life cycle cost is spent on maintenance activities — not on initial development. Software is typically built in 1–2 years but maintained for 15–20 years or longer — the maintenance period is 10× longer than development. Approximately 60% of maintenance effort is perfective (enhancements and improvements) — not bug fixing — so budgets based only on corrective work are severely inadequate. Each maintenance change touches code that other modules depend on — ripple effects increase analysis and regression testing effort. Original developers leave; new maintainers must rebuild understanding from code and incomplete documentation — familiarity (D) starts low. Complexity (C) grows with every change unless preventive refactoring is performed — Lehman's Law of Increasing Complexity applies. Legacy systems use obsolete languages and platforms — specialised skills are scarce and expensive. Undocumented changes accumulate over years — each new maintainer spends extra effort discovering what previous teams changed. Organisations that under-budget maintenance face escalating technical debt and eventual system failure or forced replacement. Components of maintenance cost and effort Cost componentWhat it includes LaborSalaries of maintenance engineers, analysts, testers, project managers — the largest single cost component Hardware and software toolsDevelopment environments, test servers, SCM tools, deployment platforms, software licences TrainingOnboarding maintainers to unfamiliar codebases, languages, or domain knowledge lost when original team leaves Time and resourcesEffort to identify the problem, design the fix, implement, test, and deploy the solution Communication and coordinationMeetings with users, management, and other teams; status reporting; change approval cycles Testing and validationRetesting the fix, regression testing unaffected areas, acceptance testing with users Maintenance effort activities include: problem analysis, feasibility study, design modification, coding, unit testing, integration testing, system testing, acceptance testing, and documentation update. Indirect costs include production downtime during failed deployments, customer support for maintenance incidents, and opportunity cost of staff not available for new product development. Maintenance effort formula (Boehm) Boehm's model relates total maintenance effort to productive baseline effort, system complexity, and team familiarity: M = P + Ke(C − D) SymbolMeaningEffect on M MTotal maintenance effort (person-months or person-hours)Output — what we estimate PProductive effort — baseline effort for a well-designed, well-documented system with experienced teamFixed baseline; lower P = better initial quality KeEmpirical constant reflecting organisation and environment (typically 4–8)Higher Ke = steeper penalty for complexity CComplexity measure — increases with poor design, high coupling, lack of documentation, large codebaseHigher C → higher M DFamiliarity measure — increases as maintainers gain knowledge of the system over timeHigher D → lower M When C > D, the term Ke(C − D) adds penalty effort — legacy systems with poor docs have high C and low D, maximising this penalty. As maintainers gain experience over months/years, D increases and M decreases for similar types of changes. Preventive maintenance (refactoring, documentation) reduces C — directly lowering future M. Staff turnover resets D — each new maintainer starts with low familiarity, increasing cost. Worked examples — Boehm formula boehm_effort_examples.pseudo // Formula: M = P + Ke * (C - D) // Example 1 — Well-maintained system, experienced team P = 40, Ke = 5, C = 4, D = 3 M = 40 + 5 * (4 - 3) = 40 + 5 = 45 person-months // Example 2 — Legacy system, poor docs, new team (EXAM FAVOURITE) P = 50, Ke = 6, C = 8, D = 3 M = 50 + 6 * (8 - 3) = 50 + 30 = 80 person-months // Example 3 — Same legacy system after 2 years (team gained familiarity) P = 50, Ke = 6, C = 8, D = 6 M = 50 + 6 * (8 - 6) = 50 + 12 = 62 person-months // Example 4 — After preventive refactoring (complexity reduced) P = 50, Ke = 6, C = 5, D = 6 M = 50 + 6 * (5 - 6) = 50 + (-6) = 44 person-months // C < D → penalty term is negative → M < P (ideal state) COCOMO maintenance effort estimation COCOMO provides a top-down estimate of annual maintenance effort based on original development size: AME = ACT × SDE AME = Annual Maintenance Effort (person-months per year) ACT (Annual Change Traffic): The fraction of the software modified per year — e.g. ACT = 0.15 means 15% of the codebase changes annually. SDE (Software Development Effort): The original total effort in person-months to develop the software — obtained from Basic COCOMO: E = a × (KLOC)b. See §3.6 COCOMO Model for development effort formulas and project type constants. Full COCOMO maintenance example cocomo_maintenance_calculation.pseudo // Step 1 — Estimate original development effort (Basic COCOMO, Organic) KLOC = 100 a = 2.4, b = 1.05 SDE = a * (KLOC ^ b) // SDE = 2.4 * (100 ^ 1.05) = 2.4 * 125.9 ≈ 302 person-months // Step 2 — Annual Maintenance Effort ACT = 0.15 // 15% of codebase changed per year AME = ACT * SDE // AME = 0.15 * 302 = 45.3 person-months per year // Step 3 — Multi-year maintenance projection maintenance_years = 10 total_maintenance = AME * maintenance_years // total = 45.3 * 10 = 453 person-months over 10 years // Step 4 — Compare to development // Development: 302 PM (one-time) // 10-year maintenance: 453 PM // Maintenance exceeds development cost in ~6.7 years: // breakeven_years = SDE / AME = 302 / 45.3 ≈ 6.7 years // Step 5 — Boehm check on a specific change cycle P = 45, Ke = 6, C = 7, D = 4 M = P + Ke * (C - D) = 45 + 6 * 3 = 63 person-months for this cycle Software maintenance cost factors (10 factors) Factors that distinguish maintenance from development and lead to higher maintenance cost: #FactorEffect on cost 1Complexity of systemMore complex → more analysis, testing, and ripple effects per change 2Size of systemLarger codebase → more modules to understand and retest 3Number of usersMore users → more change requests, higher impact of failures, more testing scenarios 4Change rateMore frequent changes → more cumulative complexity and regression risk 5Availability of skilled personnelScarce skills → higher labour cost and longer diagnosis time 6Tools and technologiesGood SCM, automated testing, CI/CD reduce cost; obsolete tools increase it 7Maintenance planClear plan with SLAs and priorities enables efficient resource use 8Age of systemOlder systems use outdated technology; harder to find skilled maintainers 9Type of maintenanceCorrective typically less expensive per change than perfective (broader scope) 10Location / labour costGeographic location affects labour rates for maintenance teams Non-technical factors FactorHow it increases maintenance cost Application domainSafety-critical domains (healthcare, aviation, finance) require rigorous change processes, formal testing, and audit trails — each change takes longer Staff stabilityHigh turnover reduces familiarity (D in Boehm formula) — each new maintainer spends months learning the system before becoming productive Program lifetimeLonger-lived systems accumulate more changes, more complexity, and more undocumented modifications over decades Dependence on external environmentSystems tightly coupled to external APIs, platforms, or regulations require frequent adaptive maintenance Hardw

title: 10.6 Software Re-engineering
headings: Re-engineering vs reuse vs new development
excerpt: Topic 10 — 10.6 Software Re-engineering 10.6 Software Re-engineering ← Topic 10 Software re-engineering is a structured approach to improving legacy systems by examining and altering them to reconstitute the system in a new form with better maintainability. It is distinct from new development and from simple reuse — see §12.1 Reusability for reuse concepts and §10.7 Reverse Engineering for the rev
full_text: Topic 10 — 10.6 Software Re-engineering 10.6 Software Re-engineering ← Topic 10 Software re-engineering is a structured approach to improving legacy systems by examining and altering them to reconstitute the system in a new form with better maintainability. It is distinct from new development and from simple reuse — see §12.1 Reusability for reuse concepts and §10.7 Reverse Engineering for the reverse engineering step within re-engineering. Definition and scope Re-engineering involves examining and altering a system to reconstitute it in a new form while preserving (or deliberately improving) external functionality. The primary goal is to improve maintainability — reducing complexity, improving structure, and updating technology without rewriting from scratch. Re-engineering is not the same as new development — it reuses existing artifacts (code, data, business logic) rather than starting from a blank requirements document. Re-engineering is also distinct from reuse, which incorporates existing components into new systems rather than transforming an existing system in place. Software re-engineering process — a structured approach to transforming legacy systems while reusing existing artifacts. Objectives of re-engineering Cost-effective evolution: Extend the useful life of a legacy system at lower cost than full replacement. Improve quality and maintainability: Restructure code, update documentation, and modernise architecture to reduce future maintenance effort. Support new platforms: Migrate the system to modern hardware, operating systems, or cloud environments. Add features: Incorporate new functionality into a restructured codebase that can accommodate change more easily. Re-engineering addresses Lehman's law of increasing complexity by actively reducing structural complexity — see §10.2. Re-engineering process phases Planning: Assess the legacy system, define re-engineering scope, estimate cost and schedule, and obtain stakeholder approval. Analysis: Understand the current system through reverse engineering, inventory analysis, and document reconstruction. Design: Define the target architecture and design the transformed system structure. Implementation: Reconstruct code and data according to the new design using forward engineering techniques. Testing: Verify that the re-engineered system preserves required functionality and meets quality standards. Deployment: Replace or parallel-run the legacy system with the re-engineered version in the production environment. Six steps of software re-engineering — from inventory analysis through forward engineering. Six steps of re-engineering Inventory Analysis: Catalogue all software assets — modules, data stores, interfaces, and documentation — to determine re-engineering candidates and scope. Document Reconstruction: Recreate missing or outdated design and requirements documentation from existing code and stakeholder knowledge. Reverse Engineering: Analyse existing code to recover design models, data structures, and control flow — see §10.7. Code Reconstruction: Restructure or rewrite code modules to improve readability, modularity, and adherence to modern standards without changing external behaviour. Data Reconstruction: Restructure databases and data files — normalise schemas, eliminate redundancy, migrate to new DBMS platforms. Forward Engineering: Apply modern design and implementation techniques to produce the transformed system from recovered specifications — see §10.7 Forward vs Reverse Engineering. Re-engineering vs reuse vs new development ApproachStarting pointOutcome Re-engineering Existing legacy system Same system transformed in place — improved structure, same business function Reuse Existing components or COTS products New system built by assembling reusable parts — see §12.1 New development Fresh requirements specification Entirely new system; legacy system retired Re-engineering cost factors Quality of existing software: Well-structured legacy code is cheaper to re-engineer than spaghetti code with no documentation. Tool support: Automated reverse engineering and refactoring tools reduce manual analysis effort. Extent of data conversion: Migrating large, complex databases to new schemas or platforms adds significant cost. Availability of expert staff: Maintainers who understand the legacy domain and technology reduce analysis time and error risk. Re-engineering should be undertaken only when cost-benefit analysis shows it is cheaper than continued maintenance or full replacement. Re-engineering steps reengineering_steps.pseudo // Six-step re-engineering process STEP 1: INVENTORY_ANALYSIS catalogue all modules, data stores, interfaces, documentation identify candidates for re-engineering vs retirement STEP 2: DOCUMENT_RECONSTRUCTION recreate missing design/requirements docs from code + interviews STEP 3: REVERSE_ENGINEERING analyse code → recover design models, data flow, control flow // see §10.7 reverse_engineering_steps.pseudo STEP 4: CODE_RECONSTRUCTION restructure modules; apply modern coding standards preserve external behaviour (regression tests required) STEP 5: DATA_RECONSTRUCTION normalise schemas; migrate to target DBMS; validate data integrity STEP 6: FORWARD_ENGINEERING implement target architecture from recovered specifications deploy re-engineered system; parallel-run with legacy until validated Exam question — Software Re-engineering Q: Define software re-engineering. How does it differ from new development and reuse? List the six re-engineering steps and process phases. What factors affect re-engineering cost? Model answer points: Definition: examine and alter system to reconstitute in new form; improve maintainability; reuse existing artifacts. Not new development (reuses artifacts); not reuse (transforms existing system in place, not assemble new from parts). Objectives: cost-effective evolution, improve quality/maintainability, new platforms, add features. Process phases: Planning → Analysis → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment. Six steps: Inventory Analysis → Document Reconstruction → Reverse Engineering → Code Reconstruction → Data Reconstruction → Forward Engineering. Cost factors: software quality, tool support, data conversion extent, expert staff availability. ← 10.5 Cost of Maintenance 10.7 Reverse Engineering →

title: 10.7 Reverse Engineering
headings: Reverse engineering in the re-engineering process
excerpt: Topic 10 — 10.7 Reverse Engineering 10.7 Reverse Engineering ← Topic 10 Reverse engineering (also called back engineering) is the process of analysing existing software to recover design information and requirements from source code or executables. It is a critical step within the re-engineering process — see §10.6 Software Re-engineering — and supports reuse decisions described in §12.1 Reusabili
full_text: Topic 10 — 10.7 Reverse Engineering 10.7 Reverse Engineering ← Topic 10 Reverse engineering (also called back engineering) is the process of analysing existing software to recover design information and requirements from source code or executables. It is a critical step within the re-engineering process — see §10.6 Software Re-engineering — and supports reuse decisions described in §12.1 Reusability. Definition Reverse engineering recovers design models, data structures, control flow, and functional specifications from existing code through systematic analysis. It moves from implementation (code) back to design and requirements — the opposite direction of normal forward engineering. Reverse engineering does not modify the software; it produces documentation and models that enable informed maintenance, re-engineering, or reuse decisions. It is also called back engineering because it reverses the usual development direction. Reverse engineering — analysing existing code to recover design information and documentation. Why reverse engineering is needed Lost documentation: Original design documents were never created or have been lost over the system's lifetime. Maintenance assistance: Maintainers need to understand module interactions and data flows before making safe changes. Reuse: Identifying reusable components within legacy systems for incorporation into new products — see §12.1. Discover flaws: Analysis may reveal design defects, security vulnerabilities, or logic errors not visible from user-level testing. Security analysis: Examining code for vulnerabilities, backdoors, or compliance violations in third-party or legacy components. Reverse engineering flow — from source code through structured analysis to recovered design documentation. Eight steps of reverse engineering Collect information: Gather all available source code, executables, documentation, and stakeholder knowledge about the system. Examine: Review the collected material to understand overall system scope, technology stack, and major components. Extract structure: Identify modules, classes, procedures, and their hierarchical relationships using static analysis tools. Record functionality: Document what each module or component does — its inputs, outputs, and processing logic. Record data flow: Map how data moves between modules — data dictionaries, variable usage, and inter-module data passing. Record control flow: Document decision points, loops, and call sequences that govern program execution paths. Review recovered design: Validate the recovered models against known behaviour, test cases, and stakeholder knowledge. Generate documentation: Produce formal design documents, structure charts, DFDs, or UML diagrams from the recovered information. Forward vs reverse engineering Forward engineering — the standard development direction from requirements to implementation. AspectForward EngineeringReverse Engineering DirectionRequirements → Design → CodeCode → Design → Requirements Starting pointRequirements specificationExisting source code or executables OutputWorking software productDesign models and documentation Modifies softwareYes — creates or extends codeNo — analyses existing code without modification When usedNormal development and re-engineering implementation phaseLegacy system analysis, maintenance, re-engineering analysis phase ToolsIDEs, compilers, CASE tools for designStatic analysers, decompilers, Rigi, Bunch, GEN++ GoalBuild a system that meets requirementsUnderstand an existing system to enable maintenance or transformation Data reverse engineering Program level: Recovering internal data structures — records, arrays, linked lists, and object attributes — from source code analysis. System level: Recovering file layouts, database schemas, and inter-system data exchange formats from files, DBMS catalogues, and log analysis. Data reverse engineering is essential before data reconstruction in the re-engineering process — see §10.6 Step 5. Processing reverse engineering Processing reverse engineering recovers the computational logic and control structures of a system at multiple levels of abstraction. Levels of abstraction: Machine code → assembly → source code → module structure → architectural design. Component analysis: Identifies individual processing components, their interfaces, and dependencies within the overall system architecture. Automated tools traverse call graphs and dependency trees to build structure models from large codebases. Reverse engineering tools Rigi: Visualisation tool for extracting and displaying software structure from source code — builds dependency graphs. Bunch: Clustering tool that groups related modules to reveal architectural layers in unstructured code. GEN++: Generates C++ class hierarchy and relationship diagrams from existing C++ source code. PBS (Portable Bookkeeper System): Tracks software metrics and structure across large multi-language codebases. CIAO: Component-based analysis tool for understanding object-oriented system architecture and dependencies. Modern equivalents include static analysers (SonarQube, Understand), decompilers, and architecture discovery tools integrated into IDEs. Reverse engineering in the re-engineering process Reverse engineering is Step 3 of the six-step re-engineering process — it provides the design input for code and data reconstruction. Without reverse engineering, re-engineering teams operate blind — making structural changes without understanding existing dependencies. Recovered documentation from reverse engineering feeds directly into forward engineering during the re-engineering implementation phase. Reverse engineering steps reverse_engineering_steps.pseudo // Eight-step reverse engineering process STEP 1: COLLECT_INFORMATION gather source code, executables, existing docs, stakeholder input STEP 2: EXAMINE review materials; identify technology stack, scope, major components STEP 3: EXTRACT_STRUCTURE use static analysis → module hierarchy, class diagrams, call graphs STEP 4: RECORD_FUNCTIONALITY FOR each module DO document inputs, outputs, processing logic END FOR STEP 5: RECORD_DATA_FLOW map data dictionaries, inter-module data passing, file/DB schemas STEP 6: RECORD_CONTROL_FLOW document decisions, loops, call sequences, state transitions STEP 7: REVIEW_DESIGN validate recovered models against known behaviour and test cases STEP 8: GENERATE_DOCUMENTATION produce design docs, DFDs, structure charts, UML diagrams // output feeds forward engineering in re-engineering (§10.6) Exam question — Reverse Engineering Q: Define reverse engineering. Why is it needed? List the eight steps. Compare forward and reverse engineering. Name reverse engineering tools. Model answer points: Definition: recover design and requirements from code analysis; back engineering; does not modify software. Why: lost documentation, maintenance assistance, reuse, discover flaws, security analysis. 8 steps: Collect info → Examine → Extract structure → Record functionality → Record data flow → Record control flow → Review design → Generate documentation. Forward: requirements→design→code, creates software; Reverse: code→design→requirements, produces documentation. Data RE: program level (internal structures) and system level (files/DB); Processing RE: levels of abstraction, component analysis. Tools: Rigi, Bunch, GEN++, PBS, CIAO. Used as Step 3 in re-engineering process (§10.6). ← 10.6 Software Re-engineering Key takeaways →

title: Key takeaways — exam revision
excerpt: Topic 10 — Key takeaways Key takeaways — exam revision ← Topic 10 10.1 Overview: IEEE: modification after delivery to correct faults, improve performance/attributes, adapt to modified environment. ISO/IEC 12207: activities when code/docs modified due to problem or improvement/adaptation. Continuous throughout lifecycle; part of SDLC maintenance phase (§2.1). Key aspects: Bug fixing, enhancements,
full_text: Topic 10 — Key takeaways Key takeaways — exam revision ← Topic 10 10.1 Overview: IEEE: modification after delivery to correct faults, improve performance/attributes, adapt to modified environment. ISO/IEC 12207: activities when code/docs modified due to problem or improvement/adaptation. Continuous throughout lifecycle; part of SDLC maintenance phase (§2.1). Key aspects: Bug fixing, enhancements, performance optimization, porting/migration, re-engineering, documentation. Planned vs unplanned; proactive vs reactive. Stakeholders: original dev team, in-house maintenance, third-party. ISO maintenance process (7 phases): Problem Identification → Analysis → Design → Implementation → System Test → Acceptance Test → Delivery. All changes via change control (§9.5). 10.2 Evolutionary entity: Software models real world; real-world changes require software changes. E-type = evolutionary (business systems); S-type = static (fixed spec). Software never finished: build 1–2 years, maintain 15–20 years. Lehman's 8 laws: Continuing Change, Increasing Complexity, Self Regulation, Conservation of Organisational Stability, Conservation of Familiarity, Continuing Growth, Declining Quality, Feedback System. Legacy systems: Old operational software with outdated tech, poor docs, high cost; options: maintain, re-engineer, replace, retire. 10.3 Need: Correct faults, improve design, enhancements, interface with other systems, new hardware/OS, migrate legacy, retire software, user requirement changes. Challenges: 10–15 year lifespan, undocumented changes, legacy code, complexity, changing requirements, interoperability, lack of tests, skill gaps, high cost. Support processes: Maintenance plan, SCM (Topic 9), change control (§9.5), regression testing (Topic 7), current documentation. 10.4 Categories (IMPORTANT): IEEE four categories by purpose. Corrective = fix discovered problems (least expensive per change; retest + regression). Adaptive = changed environment, same function (OS port, regulations). Perfective = improve performance/features/restructure (~60% effort). Preventive = correct latent faults before failure; reduces C in effort formula. Proactive vs reactive: Proactive = scheduled preventive/planned; Reactive = after failure (corrective/emergency). Patching = emergency corrective; needs impact analysis; risk of new bugs. Exam traps: OS port = adaptive (NOT perfective); new feature = perfective (NOT corrective); refactor = preventive. UGC NET 2016: i–b, ii–c, iii–d, iv–a → Answer B. Memory: C=Correct bugs, A=Adapt environment, P=Perfect/improve, P=Prevent latent faults. 10.5 Cost (IMPORTANT): >65% lifecycle cost; build 1–2 yrs, maintain 15–20 yrs. M = P + Ke(C−D): legacy example P=50,Ke=6,C=8,D=3 → M=80 PM; after familiarity D=6 → M=62 PM; after refactoring C=5 → M=44 PM. COCOMO maintenance: AME = ACT × SDE. Example: 100 KLOC organic → SDE≈302 PM; ACT=0.15 → AME=45.3 PM/yr; breakeven ~6.7 years. Corrective cheaper per change than perfective. 10 cost factors: Complexity, size, users, change rate, personnel, tools, plan, age, maintenance type, location. Non-technical: domain, staff stability, lifetime, external dependence, hardware stability. Technical: module independence, language, style, testing, docs, SCM. Postponing maintenance: Customer dissatisfaction, hidden errors, quality decline, technical debt, security exposure, staff turnover (reduces D). Reduce cost: design for maintainability, proactive strategy, automation, Agile/DevOps, SCM, re-engineering. 10.6 Re-engineering: Examine and alter system to reconstitute in new form; improve maintainability; reuse existing artifacts. Not new development; not reuse (§12.1). Process: Planning → Analysis → Design → Implementation → Testing → Deployment. Six re-engineering steps: Inventory Analysis → Document Reconstruction → Reverse Engineering → Code Reconstruction → Data Reconstruction → Forward Engineering. Cost factors: software quality, tool support, data conversion, expert staff. 10.7 Reverse engineering: Recover design/requirements from code; back engineering; does not modify software. Why: lost docs, maintenance, reuse, discover flaws, security analysis. 8 reverse engineering steps: Collect info → Examine → Extract structure → Record functionality → Record data flow → Record control flow → Review design → Generate documentation. Forward vs reverse: Forward = requirements→design→code (creates software); Reverse = code→design→requirements (produces documentation). Data RE: program level + system level. Tools: Rigi, Bunch, GEN++, PBS, CIAO. ← 10.7 Reverse Engineering Topic 11 →
