title: 11.1 Software Reliability
excerpt: Topic 11 — 11.1 Software Reliability 11.1 Software Reliability ← Topic 11 Software reliability is the probability that software will operate failure-free for a specified period of time in a specified environment. It is a core quality attribute in McCall's model and ISO/IEC 9126 — see §8.5 Quality Frameworks — and must be specified in the SRS as a non-functional requirement — see §4.9 Requirements
full_text: Topic 11 — 11.1 Software Reliability 11.1 Software Reliability ← Topic 11 Software reliability is the probability that software will operate failure-free for a specified period of time in a specified environment. It is a core quality attribute in McCall's model and ISO/IEC 9126 — see §8.5 Quality Frameworks — and must be specified in the SRS as a non-functional requirement — see §4.9 Requirements Classification. Definition Software reliability measures how consistently a system performs its intended functions without failure over time. It is expressed as a probability — the likelihood of failure-free operation during a fixed interval in a fixed environment. Reliability is measured per some unit of time (hours, days, execution cycles) rather than as a one-time pass/fail check. Software reliability depends entirely on human factors in design — it cannot be predicted from any physical basis like hardware wear. Software reliability lifecycle — the system starts with many faults, enters a useful life after testing and debugging, and may acquire new faults when upgrades are applied. Software reliability lifecycle Software begins with many faults when first created — defects are introduced during requirements, design, and coding. After testing and debugging, the system enters a useful life phase with fewer failures. Upgrades and enhancements during useful life can introduce new faults, requiring further testing. There is no equivalent of hardware preventive maintenance — software does not wear out, but it can become unreliable through changes and environment shifts. Unlike hardware, software failures are not time-dependent in the wear-and-tear sense — a fault dormant today may trigger tomorrow when new input or environment conditions appear. Hardware reliability vs software reliability Hardware reliability follows the bathtub curve — infant mortality, useful life, and wear-out phases driven by physical deterioration. FeatureHardware ReliabilitySoftware Reliability Source of failureDefects in design, production, and maintenanceDefects in design (and specification) Wear and tearFailures occur due to physical deteriorationNo wear and tear — faults do not age physically Failure warningPrior deterioration warning before failureNo prior deterioration warning Failure curveBathtub curve appliesNo bathtub curve for failure rates Time dependenceFailures are time-dependent (wear-out)Failures are not time-dependent in the physical sense Prediction from designReliability can be predicted from physical designCannot be predicted from physical basis alone Environment impactStrongly related to environmental conditionsExternal environment does not directly degrade code RedundancyHardware redundancy has physical limitsSoftware redundancy (N-version, recovery blocks) can improve reliability MaintenanceRepairs can make hardware more reliableNo preventive maintenance equivalent — fixes require re-testing MetricOften expressed as MTBFMeasured using ROCOF, MTTF, POFOD, availability, etc. Reliability attributes (measures) Reliability attributes quantify software dependability and should be specified in the SRS so achievement can be verified objectively. A good reliability measure should be observer-independent — different people should obtain the same value when measuring the same system. AttributeMeaning ROCOF (Rate of Occurrence of Failure)Frequency of unexpected behaviour — how many times the software fails per unit time; total failures divided by observation interval. MTTF (Mean Time To Failure)Average time between successive failures during execution; only run time is counted — downtime for repair is excluded. MTTR (Mean Time To Repair)Average time to locate and fix the error that caused a failure. MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)MTBF = MTTF + MTTR; time between one failure and the next, including repair time (real time, not execution time). POFOD (Probability of Failure on Demand)Probability the system fails when a service request is made; e.g. POFOD = 0.001 means 1 failure per 1000 requests. AvailabilityProbability the system is available for use over a given period; considers both failure frequency and downtime during repair. Fault toleranceAbility to continue functioning correctly despite unexpected events or component breakdowns — see §11.2 Fault Tolerance. RecoverabilityAbility to bounce back quickly from a setback with minimal downtime and data loss. RedundancyDuplication of critical components to avoid single points of failure and maintain service during faults. MTTF calculation example mttf_calculation.pseudo // Failures observed at execution time instants (hours of run time) T = [10, 25, 40, 58, 70] // T1..T5 failure times n = length(T) - 1 // 4 intervals between 5 failures // MTTF = average of intervals between successive failures intervals = [] FOR i = 1 TO n DO intervals.add(T[i+1] - T[i]) END FOR MTTF = sum(intervals) / n // intervals = [15, 15, 18, 12] → MTTF = 60/4 = 15 hours // MTTR = average repair time (real time, not execution time) repair_times = [2, 3, 1, 4] // hours to fix each failure MTTR = sum(repair_times) / length(repair_times) // = 2.5 hours MTBF = MTTF + MTTR // = 15 + 2.5 = 17.5 hours (real time between failures) Factors affecting software reliability Complexity: High software complexity is a major contributor to reliability problems — McCabe's cyclomatic complexity is one complexity-oriented metric; see §6.6 Cyclomatic Complexity. Optimistic assumption: Assuming software that runs correctly once will always run correctly leads to hidden defects surfacing later under new conditions. Environment change: Software that worked in one environment may fail when the operating environment changes — e.g. the Ariane 5 rocket failure caused by design defects exposed by faster horizontal drift speed not present in Ariane 4. Specification and coding errors: Failures arise from ambiguities, oversights, misinterpretation of specifications, inadequate testing, and incorrect or unexpected usage. No universal model: Many software reliability models exist, but no single model captures all complexity — each requires assumptions and fits specific contexts; see §11.3 Reliability Growth Models. Achieving high reliability — four strategies Error avoidance: Prevent errors during development using experienced developers, appropriate software engineering tools, and suitable CASE tools. Error detection: Find remaining errors through rigorous testing — unit, integration, system, acceptance, and reliability testing. Error removal: Fix detected errors and re-test repeatedly until the error is confirmed removed — iterative reliability testing. Fault tolerance: When 100% error-free software is impractical, design the system to deliver correct results despite residual failures — N-version programming, recovery blocks, rollback recovery; see §11.2. Design for reliability from the start — include fault tolerance, redundancy, and error handling in the architecture. Maintain high code quality through modularization, encapsulation, and abstraction for easier maintenance and updates. Document the system thoroughly so it can be maintained and tested consistently over time. Apply continuous improvement based on user feedback and ongoing testing to adapt to changing requirements. Design with security in mind — encryption, access controls, and secure coding practices support overall dependability. Exam question — Software Reliability Q: Define software reliability. List reliability attributes with formulas. Differentiate hardware and software reliability. Explain how high reliability is achieved. Model answer points: Software reliability = probability of failure-free operation for fixed time in fixed environment; human-design dependent; no physical wear. Lifecycle: many initial faults → testing/debugging → useful life → upgrades may add new faults. Attributes: ROCOF, MTTF (execution time only), MTTR, MTBF = MTTF + MTTR, POFOD, availability, fault tolerance, recoverability. Hardware vs s

title: 11.2 Fault Tolerance
headings: Failure probability — recovery block vs N-version programming
RB sequential vs NVP parallel — summary
excerpt: Topic 11 — 11.2 Fault Tolerance 11.2 Fault Tolerance ← Topic 11 Fault tolerance is the ability of a system to continue delivering correct service despite the presence of faults. It is one of the reliability attributes introduced in §11.1 Software Reliability and is achieved when 100% error-free software is impractical — the system is designed to detect, contain, and recover from failures rather th
full_text: Topic 11 — 11.2 Fault Tolerance 11.2 Fault Tolerance ← Topic 11 Fault tolerance is the ability of a system to continue delivering correct service despite the presence of faults. It is one of the reliability attributes introduced in §11.1 Software Reliability and is achieved when 100% error-free software is impractical — the system is designed to detect, contain, and recover from failures rather than prevent every defect. Definition Fault tolerance means the system can operate correctly (or degrade gracefully) even when one or more components fail or produce incorrect results. A fault is a defect in the system; an error is the manifestation of a fault; a failure is when the error causes incorrect external behaviour. Fault tolerance does not eliminate faults — it prevents faults from propagating into observable failures. It is essential in safety-critical, real-time, and high-availability systems where downtime or incorrect output is unacceptable. Basic fault tolerant techniques Redundancy: Duplicate hardware or software components so that a backup can take over when the primary fails. Checkpointing: Save the system state at intervals so execution can be rolled back to a known-good point after a failure. Error detection and correction: Use checksums, parity bits, voting, or exception handlers to detect and correct erroneous outputs. Failure prediction: Monitor system behaviour to anticipate impending failures and trigger preventive action before a fault becomes a failure. Load balancing: Distribute workload across multiple replicas so that the failure of one node does not halt the entire service. Isolation: Confine faults within a module or process boundary so they cannot corrupt unrelated components. Replication: Maintain multiple copies of data or services; if one replica fails, others continue serving requests. Dynamic reconfiguration: Reorganise the system at runtime — reroute traffic, activate standby components, or reassign tasks when a fault is detected. Fault removal vs fault masking Fault removal eliminates the underlying defect through debugging and re-testing — the fault no longer exists in the code. Fault masking hides the effect of a fault at runtime without removing it — the fault may still be present but its incorrect output is overridden or corrected. Recovery blocks and N-version programming are fault-masking techniques: they deliver a correct result despite a faulty version executing. Fault removal is preferred during development; fault masking is used at runtime when residual faults cannot be eliminated before deployment. Forward vs backward error recovery Backward error recovery restores the system to a previously saved checkpoint and re-executes from that point — used in checkpoint/rollback schemes. Forward error recovery continues execution from the current state by applying corrective action — used in recovery blocks and N-version programming where an alternative version produces the correct result. Backward recovery requires saving and restoring state, which adds storage and time overhead but guarantees a clean restart. Forward recovery avoids rollback overhead but requires redundant computation paths (alternate versions or acceptance tests). Design diversity Design diversity means implementing the same specification using independent teams, languages, algorithms, or compilers so that correlated faults are unlikely. The goal is to ensure that versions fail independently — a bug in one version should not appear in the others. It underpins both recovery blocks and N-version programming, where multiple diverse implementations of the same function are maintained. Complete independence is difficult to achieve in practice because teams may share specifications and test cases, introducing common-mode failures. Recovery block scheme (RB) In the recovery block scheme, versions are tried sequentially. The primary version runs first; if its output fails the acceptance test, the system falls back to alternate versions one at a time until one passes or all are exhausted. Recovery block scheme — sequential trial of primary and alternate versions with acceptance testing. The recovery block syntax is: recovery_block.syntax Ensure T By P Else by Q1 Else by Q2 ... Else by Qn Else Error T is the acceptance test that validates the output of each version. P is the primary version; Q1, Q2, …, Qn are diverse alternate versions. Versions are executed one after another — only one version runs at a time until a passing result is found. If all versions fail the acceptance test, the Error clause is triggered. N-version programming (NVP) In N-version programming, N independent versions execute in parallel on identical inputs. A voter selects the majority result (or applies a more sophisticated selection rule) to produce the final output. N-version programming — parallel execution of diverse versions with majority voting. All N versions run simultaneously, so NVP has higher computational cost than recovery blocks but lower latency on the success path. The voter compares outputs and selects the result agreed upon by at least two versions (for N = 3). NVP masks faults as long as a majority of versions produce the correct output. Common-mode failures across all versions defeat NVP — design diversity must be genuine. Checkpoint and rollback recovery Checkpoint/rollback recovery — periodic state saves enable backward error recovery after a failure. The system periodically saves its state (checkpoint) to stable storage. When a failure is detected, execution rolls back to the most recent valid checkpoint and resumes from there. This is a backward error recovery technique — it does not require diverse versions but does require idempotent or repeatable computation after rollback. Checkpoint frequency trades storage overhead against the amount of work lost on rollback. Failure probability — recovery block vs N-version programming Let p be the probability that a single version fails (produces incorrect output or fails the acceptance test). Assume versions fail independently. Recovery block (sequential, k versions): All k versions must fail for the block to fail. P(RB fails) = pk. N-version programming (parallel, N = 3, majority vote): NVP fails when at least 2 of 3 versions fail. P(NVP fails) = 3p²(1 − p) + p³ = 3p² − 2p³. For p = 0.01: P(RB fails with k = 3) = 10⁻⁶; P(NVP fails with N = 3) ≈ 2.98 × 10⁻⁴ — RB is more reliable for the same number of versions when p is small. For p = 0.1: P(RB fails with k = 3) = 0.001; P(NVP fails with N = 3) = 0.028 — RB still wins, but the gap narrows as p increases. NVP trades higher parallel cost for lower response time; RB trades sequential latency for lower resource usage. Recovery block — pseudocode recovery_block.pseudo // Recovery block: sequential trial with acceptance test versions = [P, Q1, Q2, Q3] // primary + alternates acceptance_test = T FOR each version v IN versions DO result = EXECUTE(v, input) IF acceptance_test(result) THEN RETURN result // forward recovery — correct output found END IF END FOR RAISE Error // all versions failed acceptance test N-version programming — pseudocode n_version_programming.pseudo // N-version programming: parallel execution with majority vote versions = [V1, V2, V3] // N = 3 diverse implementations results = [] PARALLEL FOR each version v IN versions DO results.add(EXECUTE(v, input)) END PARALLEL // Majority voter — select result agreed by at least 2 versions FOR each candidate r IN results DO count = COUNT(results WHERE result == r) IF count >= 2 THEN RETURN r // forward recovery via majority END IF END FOR RAISE Error // no majority agreement — all disagree or 2+ failed RB sequential vs NVP parallel — summary FeatureRecovery Block (RB)N-Version Programming (NVP) ExecutionSequential — one version at a timeParallel — all versions simultaneously Recovery typeForward error recoveryForward error recovery (majority vote) Resource costLower — only one version active at a timeHigher — all N v

title: 11.3 Reliability Growth Models
excerpt: Topic 11 — 11.3 Reliability Growth Models 11.3 Reliability Growth Models ← Topic 11 Software Reliability Growth Models (SRGMs) are mathematical models that describe how software reliability improves as faults are detected and removed during testing. They are introduced here as an overview; individual syllabus models are covered in §11.4 through §11.8. Definition An SRGM is a statistical model that
full_text: Topic 11 — 11.3 Reliability Growth Models 11.3 Reliability Growth Models ← Topic 11 Software Reliability Growth Models (SRGMs) are mathematical models that describe how software reliability improves as faults are detected and removed during testing. They are introduced here as an overview; individual syllabus models are covered in §11.4 through §11.8. Definition An SRGM is a statistical model that relates the number of failures (or the failure rate) to the amount of testing effort — measured in execution time or number of test cases. As testing progresses and faults are removed, the failure intensity decreases and reliability increases — this growth pattern is what the model captures. SRGMs are black-box models: they use observed failure data without requiring knowledge of the internal program structure. No single SRGM is universally valid — each model makes specific assumptions about fault detection and debugging behaviour. Reliability growth during testing — failure rate decreases as faults are detected and removed over time or test cases. What SRGMs represent SRGMs plot reliability or failure rate (intensity) against time (execution hours) or against number of test cases executed. The x-axis is cumulative testing effort; the y-axis is either the probability of failure-free operation (reliability) or the instantaneous failure rate λ(t). A typical growth curve shows high failure intensity early in testing, declining as more faults are found and fixed. The curve can be used to estimate when the software will reach a target reliability level (e.g. 0.99 over 100 hours). Purposes of SRGMs Predict reliability: Estimate the reliability the software will achieve after additional testing — e.g. "after 500 more test cases, reliability will reach 0.995." Guide testing: Determine how much more testing is needed to reach a reliability target before release. Allocate resources: Decide whether to continue testing, add staff, or release based on projected reliability versus the required threshold. Identify problem areas: A model that fits poorly may indicate inconsistent debugging, incomplete fault removal, or heterogeneous fault populations. Black-box models Black-box SRGMs treat the software as an opaque system — only failure times or failure counts are observed, not code paths or module structure. They assume faults are removed upon detection (perfect or imperfect debugging depending on the model). Parameters are estimated from test data using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) or least-squares fitting. White-box (structure-based) models use program complexity metrics but are less common in the syllabus SRGM set. Failure data types Data typeNameWhat is recordedExample Type-I Time-between-failure (TBF) Exact time intervals between successive failures during execution Failures at 10 h, 25 h, 40 h → TBF = [10, 15, 15] Type-II Failure-count Number of failures observed within fixed time intervals or after fixed test cases 3 failures in first 100 test cases, 2 in next 100 → counts = [3, 2] Type-I data is more informative because exact failure times are recorded, but it requires continuous monitoring during execution. Type-II data is easier to collect in batch testing but provides less precise timing information. Different SRGMs use different data types — JM and SW use Type-I; GO and PNZ support both Type-I and Type-II. Non-homogeneous Poisson process (NHPP) Many SRGMs are based on the Non-Homogeneous Poisson Process (NHPP), where the failure intensity λ(t) varies with time rather than remaining constant. In an NHPP, the expected number of failures by time t is given by the mean value function m(t) = ∫₀ᵗ λ(s) ds. As faults are removed, λ(t) decreases and m(t) grows more slowly — modelling reliability growth. The §3.11 Quasi Renewal Process is a related counting process where inter-failure intervals grow by a constant factor α > 1, modelling reliability growth during burn-in. Duane and Coutinho models (brief) The Duane model (1964) was originally developed for hardware reliability growth and plots cumulative failure rate against cumulative operating time on log-log axes — a straight line indicates reliability growth. The Coutinho model extends Duane's approach to software, relating failure rate to cumulative test time with a power-law relationship. Both are empirical curve-fitting models rather than stochastic process models — they are less detailed than JM, SW, GO, or PNZ but useful for quick trend analysis. Key syllabus models ModelYearTypeKey assumptionSection Jelinski-Moranda (JM) 1972 Binomial / step function Constant hazard φ per remaining fault; perfect debugging §11.4 Schick-Wolverton (SW) 1978 Modification of JM Failure rate increases with time since last debugging §11.5 Goel-Okumoto (GO) 1979 Exponential NHPP Failures proportional to remaining faults; perfect debugging §11.6 Pham-Nordmann-Zhang (PNZ) — NHPP with imperfect debugging S-shaped detection; faults introduced during debugging at rate α §11.7 Mills Error Seeding 1980s Fault content estimation Inject known errors; estimate total inherent faults via sampling §11.8 SRGM workflow — pseudocode srgm_workflow.pseudo // Step 1: Collect failure data during testing IF continuous monitoring THEN data = Type_I_time_between_failures() // exact failure times ELSE data = Type_II_failure_counts() // failures per test interval END IF // Step 2: Select an appropriate SRGM // Consider: data type, debugging assumptions, growth curve shape model = SELECT_MODEL(data) // e.g. JM, SW, GO, PNZ // Step 3: Estimate model parameters via MLE or least squares params = ESTIMATE_PARAMETERS(model, data) // e.g. JM: N (initial faults), φ (hazard rate) // e.g. GO: a (total faults), b (detection rate) // Step 4: Validate model fit (goodness-of-fit test) goodness = CHI_SQUARE_TEST(model, data, params) IF goodness FAILS THEN TRY alternative model OR collect more data END IF // Step 5: Predict reliability at future testing effort target_time = 500 // hours or test cases R_predicted = model.RELIABILITY(target_time, params) failure_rate = model.LAMBDA(target_time, params) // Step 6: Decision — release or continue testing? IF R_predicted >= reliability_target THEN RECOMMEND release ELSE extra_effort = COMPUTE_EFFORT_FOR_TARGET(model, reliability_target) RECOMMEND continue testing for extra_effort units END IF Exam question — Reliability Growth Models Q: Define SRGM. State its purposes. Differentiate Type-I and Type-II failure data. List the key syllabus models with their assumptions. Model answer points: SRGM = mathematical model relating failure rate/reliability to testing effort; black-box; reliability grows as faults are removed. Purposes: predict reliability, guide testing, allocate resources, identify problem areas. Type-I = time-between-failure (exact times); Type-II = failure-count per interval. NHPP: failure intensity λ(t) varies with time; mean value function m(t) = ∫λ(s)ds. JM (1972, binomial, perfect debugging); SW (1978, λ increases with time since debug); GO (1979, exponential NHPP); PNZ (imperfect debugging, S-shaped); Mills (error seeding). Workflow: collect data → select model → estimate parameters → validate fit → predict → decide release. ← 11.2 Fault Tolerance 11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model →

title: 11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model
excerpt: Topic 11 — 11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model 11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model ← Topic 11 The Jelinski-Moranda (JM) model (1972) is the first and one of the best-known black-box software reliability growth models. It belongs to the binomial type of SRGMs and is covered in the overview at §11.3 Reliability Growth Models. Model type The JM model is a binomial-type model — it assumes a fixed initial number of fa
full_text: Topic 11 — 11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model 11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model ← Topic 11 The Jelinski-Moranda (JM) model (1972) is the first and one of the best-known black-box software reliability growth models. It belongs to the binomial type of SRGMs and is covered in the overview at §11.3 Reliability Growth Models. Model type The JM model is a binomial-type model — it assumes a fixed initial number of faults N, and each fault has an equal constant probability of causing the next failure. It was among the earliest attempts to apply reliability growth theory (originally developed for hardware) to software testing data. The failure intensity drops in discrete steps each time a fault is detected and removed — producing a step-function reliability growth curve. Assumptions The software contains exactly N initial faults at the start of testing. Each remaining fault has the same constant hazard rate φ (phi) — the probability per unit time that a specific fault causes a failure. Failures occur independently — the failure caused by one fault does not affect the failure times of other faults. Perfect debugging: when a failure occurs, the fault that caused it is immediately and permanently removed — no new faults are introduced. Failure intensity After (i − 1) faults have been removed, (N − (i − 1)) faults remain. The failure intensity at the ith failure interval is: λ(ti) = φ [N − (i − 1)] At the start (i = 1), λ = φN — the highest failure rate when all N faults are present. After each fault is removed, λ drops by φ — a step decrease. When all N faults are removed, λ = 0 — the software is fault-free under this model's assumptions. Reliability function The probability of failure-free operation during the ith interval of duration ti (time since the (i − 1)th failure) is: R(ti) = e−φ [N − (i − 1)] ti Reliability increases as i increases (fewer remaining faults) and as ti increases (more time without failure in the current interval). The reliability curve is exponential within each interval but steps up at each fault removal. Parameters ParameterSymbolMeaning Initial fault countNTotal number of faults present at the start of testing Fault hazard rateφConstant failure rate per individual fault per unit time Both N and φ are estimated from Type-I (time-between-failure) data using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). N must be an integer ≥ the number of failures observed; φ must be positive. Characteristics CharacteristicDetail Model typeBinomial — fixed N faults, equal hazard φ Failure intensity shapeStep function — drops by φ at each fault removal Debugging assumptionPerfect — faults removed permanently, none introduced Data typeType-I (time-between-failure) ParametersN (initial faults), φ (per-fault hazard) Reliability functionR(ti) = e−φ[N−(i−1)]ti Prediction tendencyOptimistic — assumes all faults will eventually be found LimitationConstant φ per fault is unrealistic — some faults are harder to trigger than others Optimistic predictions The JM model tends to produce optimistic reliability predictions because it assumes perfect debugging and a fixed, finite fault count. In practice, debugging may introduce new faults and the total fault count is not known in advance — the model overestimates how quickly reliability will improve. Despite this limitation, JM remains important as the foundational SRGM and as a basis for later models such as the §11.5 Schick-Wolverton Model. Exam question — Jelinski-Moranda Model Q: State the JM model assumptions. Write the failure intensity and reliability formulas. Why are JM predictions optimistic? Model answer points: JM (1972): first black-box SRGM; binomial type; step-function failure intensity. Assumptions: N initial faults; constant hazard φ per fault; independent failures; perfect debugging. λ(ti) = φ[N − (i − 1)]; R(ti) = e−φ[N−(i−1)]ti. Parameters: N, φ — estimated via MLE from Type-I data. Optimistic because perfect debugging and fixed N overestimate reliability growth; constant φ unrealistic. Modified by Schick-Wolverton (1978) — see §11.5. ← 11.3 Reliability Growth Models 11.5 Schick-Wolverton Model →

title: 11.5 Schick-Wolverton Model
excerpt: Topic 11 — 11.5 Schick-Wolverton Model 11.5 Schick-Wolverton Model ← Topic 11 The Schick-Wolverton (SW) model (1978) is a modification of the §11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model. It retains JM's assumption of a fixed initial fault count N and perfect debugging, but introduces a time-dependent failure rate that increases within each failure interval. Motivation In the JM model, the failure intensity λ is
full_text: Topic 11 — 11.5 Schick-Wolverton Model 11.5 Schick-Wolverton Model ← Topic 11 The Schick-Wolverton (SW) model (1978) is a modification of the §11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model. It retains JM's assumption of a fixed initial fault count N and perfect debugging, but introduces a time-dependent failure rate that increases within each failure interval. Motivation In the JM model, the failure intensity λ is constant within each interval between failures — it only changes when a fault is removed. In practice, the longer a fault remains undetected, the more likely it is to cause a failure as more execution paths are exercised. The SW model captures this by making λ proportional to the time elapsed since the last debugging event. SW is more sophisticated than JM and generally provides better fits to real failure data. Assumptions The software contains exactly N initial faults at the start of testing. Each remaining fault has hazard rate φ, but the overall failure intensity increases linearly with time since the last fault was removed. Failures occur independently. Perfect debugging: faults are permanently removed upon detection; no new faults are introduced. Failure intensity During the ith failure interval, where ti is the time elapsed since the (i − 1)th failure was debugged: λ(ti) = φ [N − (i − 1)] ti At ti = 0 (immediately after debugging), λ = 0 — no failure can occur instantly after a fix. As ti increases, λ grows linearly — reflecting increased exposure of remaining faults. When a new failure occurs and a fault is removed, λ resets to 0 and the coefficient drops to φ[N − i]. Reliability function The probability of failure-free operation during the ith interval is: R(ti) = e−φ [N − (i − 1)] ti² / 2 The exponent contains ti² rather than ti because λ(ti) is linear in ti — integrating λ gives a ti²/2 term. Reliability decreases more rapidly early in the interval (when λ is small) and more slowly later — a different shape from JM's exponential decay. Comparison with JM FeatureJelinski-Moranda (JM)Schick-Wolverton (SW) Year19721978 Failure intensityλ(ti) = φ[N − (i − 1)] — constant within intervalλ(ti) = φ[N − (i − 1)]ti — increases with time ReliabilityR = e−φ[N−(i−1)]tiR = e−φ[N−(i−1)]ti²/2 λ at start of intervalφ[N − (i − 1)] — non-zero0 — resets after each debugging ParametersN, φN, φ Data typeType-IType-I Fit qualityOften too optimistic; poor fit to some datasetsBetter fit; more realistic time-dependent intensity Parameter estimation Both N and φ are estimated from Type-I failure data using Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE). Estimating N is particularly important — it represents the total inherent fault content and directly affects reliability predictions. The MLE for N involves maximising the likelihood function over integer values N ≥ n (where n is the number of observed failures). φ is estimated simultaneously; the log-likelihood for the SW model incorporates the ti² term from the reliability function. Exam question — Schick-Wolverton Model Q: How does the SW model modify JM? Write the failure intensity and reliability formulas. Why is SW more sophisticated? Model answer points: SW (1978) = modification of JM; same N and perfect debugging assumptions. Key change: failure rate increases with time since last debugging — λ(ti) = φ[N − (i − 1)]ti. R(ti) = e−φ[N−(i−1)]ti²/2. λ resets to 0 after each fault removal; grows linearly within interval. More sophisticated: captures time-dependent exposure; better fit than JM. Parameters N, φ estimated via MLE from Type-I data. ← 11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model 11.6 Goel-Okumoto Model →

title: 11.6 Goel-Okumoto Model
excerpt: Topic 11 — 11.6 Goel-Okumoto Model 11.6 Goel-Okumoto Model ← Topic 11 The Goel-Okumoto (GO) model (1979) is an exponential Non-Homogeneous Poisson Process (NHPP) model. Unlike the step-function §11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model, GO produces a smooth, continuously decreasing failure intensity curve. full_text: Topic 11 — 11.6 Goel-Okumoto Model 11.6 Goel-Okumoto Model ← Topic 11 The Goel-Okumoto (GO) model (1979) is an exponential Non-Homogeneous Poisson Process (NHPP) model. Unlike the step-function §11.4 Jelinski-Moranda Model, GO produces a smooth, continuously decreasing failure intensity curve. Model type GO is an NHPP model — failures occur according to a Poisson process whose rate λ(t) changes over time. It is one of the most widely used SRGMs because of its simplicity, two-parameter form, and good empirical fit. The mean value function m(t) has an exponential saturation shape — failures are detected rapidly at first, then more slowly as remaining faults decrease. Assumptions Faults in the software are independent of each other. The failure rate at any time is proportional to the number of remaining (undetected) faults. When a fault causes a failure, it is removed from the program before the next test session — faults are not rediscovered. Perfect debugging: every detected fault is permanently and correctly fixed; no new faults are introduced during debugging. Differential equation Let m(t) be the expected cumulative number of faults detected by time t, and a be the total initial fault content. The rate of fault detection is proportional to remaining faults: dm/dt = b [a − m(t)] The left side is the rate at which new faults are detected per unit time. The right side says detection rate = b × (faults not yet found) = b[a − m(t)]. As m(t) approaches a, the detection rate approaches zero — all faults have been found. Mean value function Solving the differential equation with m(0) = 0 gives: m(t) = a (1 − e−bt) At t = 0, m(0) = 0 — no faults detected yet. As t → ∞, m(t) → a — all a faults are eventually detected. The curve is S-shaped in cumulative failures vs time — rapid growth initially, then saturation. Parameters ParameterSymbolMeaning Total fault contentaTotal number of faults initially present in the software Fault detection ratebRate at which remaining faults are detected per unit time Failure intensity and reliability Failure intensity: λ(t) = dm/dt = ab e−bt — decreases exponentially over time. Reliability (probability of zero failures in [0, t]): R(t) = e−m(t) = e−a(1−e−bt). Reliability increases as testing continues because m(t) grows (more faults removed) and λ(t) shrinks. Parameter estimation (MLE) Type-I data (time-between-failure): MLE maximises the likelihood using exact failure times t₁, t₂, …, tn. The log-likelihood involves terms ln λ(ti) and m(tn). Type-II data (failure-count): MLE uses the number of failures ni observed in each time interval [ti−1, ti]. The likelihood is based on the Poisson probability of observing ni failures given m(ti) − m(ti−1). Both a and b are estimated simultaneously; iterative numerical methods (e.g. Newton-Raphson) are typically used. Initial estimates can be obtained from a plot of cumulative failures vs time — the saturation level estimates a, and the slope estimates b. Characteristics CharacteristicDetail Model typeExponential NHPP Mean value functionm(t) = a(1 − e−bt) Failure intensityλ(t) = ab e−bt — smooth exponential decay ReliabilityR(t) = e−a(1−e−bt) DebuggingPerfect — no new faults introduced Data typesBoth Type-I and Type-II Parametersa (total faults), b (detection rate) LimitationPerfect debugging assumption; exponential shape may not fit S-shaped detection phases Exam question — Goel-Okumoto Model Q: State the GO model assumptions. Write the differential equation, mean value function, and reliability formula. How are parameters estimated? Model answer points: GO (1979): exponential NHPP; smooth decreasing failure intensity. Assumptions: independent faults; failure rate ∝ remaining faults; faults removed before retest; perfect debugging. dm/dt = b[a − m(t)]; m(t) = a(1 − e−bt); λ(t) = ab e−bt. R(t) = e−a(1−e−bt). Parameters: a (total faults), b (detection rate) — MLE for Type-I and Type-II data. Contrast with JM: GO has smooth curve, not step function; supports both data types. ← 11.5 Schick-Wolverton Model 11.7 PNZ Model →

title: 11.7 PNZ Model
excerpt: Topic 11 — 11.7 PNZ Model 11.7 PNZ Model ← Topic 11 The Pham-Nordmann-Zhang (PNZ) model is an NHPP-based software reliability growth model that accounts for imperfect debugging — faults may be introduced during the debugging process. It is particularly useful for evaluating component-based systems and systems that employ §11.2 Fault Tolerance techniques. Model type PNZ is an NHPP model like the §1
full_text: Topic 11 — 11.7 PNZ Model 11.7 PNZ Model ← Topic 11 The Pham-Nordmann-Zhang (PNZ) model is an NHPP-based software reliability growth model that accounts for imperfect debugging — faults may be introduced during the debugging process. It is particularly useful for evaluating component-based systems and systems that employ §11.2 Fault Tolerance techniques. Model type PNZ is an NHPP model like the §11.6 Goel-Okumoto Model, but relaxes the perfect debugging assumption. It models an S-shaped fault detection rate — slow initial detection (faults are hard to find early), rapid middle phase, then saturation as most faults are removed. PNZ can evaluate dependability of systems built from multiple components, including those with redundant or fault-tolerant architectures. Imperfect debugging In real debugging, fixing one fault may introduce new faults — the total fault count is not fixed and may grow over time. PNZ models this by allowing the effective fault content to increase at rate α during debugging. The time-varying fault content is: a(t) = a(1 + αt), where a is the initial fault count and α is the rate at which new faults are introduced per unit time. When α = 0, PNZ reduces to a model with perfect debugging; when α > 0, the fault pool grows and reliability growth slows or reverses. Time-varying parameters Fault content function: a(t) = a(1 + αt) — total faults grow linearly with time due to imperfect debugging. Detection rate function: b(t) = b / (1 + βe−bt) — produces the S-shaped detection curve; β controls the inflection point. The S-shape arises because b(t) starts low (few faults found), rises to a peak (rapid detection phase), then declines (remaining faults are harder to find). Mean value function The expected cumulative number of faults detected by time t in the PNZ model is: m(t) = (a/b) ln[(1 + β) / (1 + βe−bt)] + αt The first term captures S-shaped detection from the initial fault pool a. The second term αt accounts for additional faults introduced during debugging that are also eventually detected. When α = 0 and β → 0, the model approaches the GO model's exponential form. Parameters ParameterSymbolMeaning Initial fault contentaNumber of faults present at the start of testing Fault detection ratebBase rate at which faults are detected S-shape controlβControls the inflection point of the S-shaped detection curve Imperfect debugging rateαRate at which new faults are introduced per unit time during debugging Failure intensity and reliability Failure intensity: λ(t) = dm/dt = ab / (1 + βe−bt) + α — combines S-shaped detection with fault introduction. Reliability: R(t) = e−m(t) — probability of zero failures in the interval [0, t]. When α > 0, reliability growth may plateau or even decrease if new faults are introduced faster than existing ones are removed. Objectives of the PNZ model Predict dependability: Estimate the reliability of component-based systems where individual components may have different fault profiles and debugging histories. Failure analysis: Identify whether reliability growth is stalling due to imperfect debugging rather than insufficient testing effort. Optimise testing: Determine the optimal amount of testing effort required to reach a reliability target, accounting for faults introduced during debugging. Cost-benefit analysis: Compare the cost of additional testing against the reliability improvement achieved, especially when debugging introduces new faults. Component-based and fault-tolerant systems PNZ can be applied to systems composed of multiple modules or components, each with its own fault content and detection rate. When combined with fault tolerance techniques (recovery blocks, N-version programming, replication), PNZ helps predict whether the system's overall dependability meets requirements despite imperfect debugging in individual components. The model's imperfect debugging parameter α is especially relevant for fault-tolerant systems where fixes to one component may affect others. Comparison with other NHPP models FeatureGoel-Okumoto (GO)PNZ DebuggingPerfect — fixed fault count aImperfect — a(t) = a(1 + αt) Detection curveExponential saturationS-shaped — b(t) = b/(1 + βe−bt) Parametersa, ba, b, β, α Mean value functionm(t) = a(1 − e−bt)m(t) = (a/b)ln[(1+β)/(1+βe−bt)] + αt Fault introductionNot modelledNew faults at rate α during debugging Use caseSimple growth predictionComponent-based systems, fault tolerance evaluation Exam question — PNZ Model Q: What is imperfect debugging in the PNZ model? Write the time-varying parameters and mean value function. State the model's objectives. Model answer points: PNZ: NHPP with imperfect debugging; S-shaped fault detection; evaluates component-based and fault-tolerant systems. Imperfect debugging: new faults introduced at rate α during debugging — a(t) = a(1 + αt). b(t) = b/(1 + βe−bt) — S-shaped detection rate. m(t) = (a/b)ln[(1+β)/(1+βe−bt)] + αt; λ(t) = ab/(1+βe−bt) + α. Parameters: a, b, β, α. Objectives: predict dependability, failure analysis, optimise testing, cost-benefit analysis. Links to fault tolerance (§11.2) for component-based dependability evaluation. ← 11.6 Goel-Okumoto Model 11.8 Mills' Error Seeding Model →

title: 11.8 Mills' Error Seeding Model
excerpt: Topic 11 — 11.8 Mills' Error Seeding Model 11.8 Mills' Error Seeding Model ← Topic 11 Mills' Error Seeding Model (1980s) is a technique for estimating the total number of inherent faults in software by injecting a known number of artificial errors and measuring how many are detected during testing. It is covered in the SRGM overview at §11.3 Reliability Growth Models as a fault-content estimation
full_text: Topic 11 — 11.8 Mills' Error Seeding Model 11.8 Mills' Error Seeding Model ← Topic 11 Mills' Error Seeding Model (1980s) is a technique for estimating the total number of inherent faults in software by injecting a known number of artificial errors and measuring how many are detected during testing. It is covered in the SRGM overview at §11.3 Reliability Growth Models as a fault-content estimation approach rather than a time-based growth model. Principle The tester injects a known number of seeded (artificial) errors into the program alongside the inherent (natural) errors already present. Testing is then performed without distinguishing seeded from inherent errors — the tester finds some of both types. The ratio of detected seeded errors to total seeded errors is assumed to equal the ratio of detected inherent errors to total inherent errors — this is the capture-recapture principle. From this ratio, the total number of inherent faults can be estimated. Variables SymbolMeaning SNumber of seeded (known) errors injected into the program sNumber of seeded errors detected during testing N₀Estimated number of inherent (natural) errors in the program n₁Total number of errors detected during testing (seeded + inherent) kNumber of inherent errors detected during testing (n₁ − s) rTotal number of errors in the combined population (S + N₀) N̂Estimated total inherent error count (rounded up) Hypergeometric distribution approach The error seeding problem is modelled using the hypergeometric distribution, which describes the probability of drawing a certain number of successes from a finite population without replacement. Population size: r = S + N₀ (seeded + inherent errors). Sample size: n₁ (total errors detected). Successes in population: S (seeded errors). Successes in sample: s (seeded errors detected). The estimate for inherent errors is: N₀ = n₁(r − k) / k − 1 The estimated total inherent error count (rounded up to the next integer) is: N̂ = ⌈N₀⌉ + 1 Here r = S + N₀ and k = n₁ − s (inherent errors found). The formula assumes seeded and inherent errors have equal probability of being detected — a critical assumption. Alternative: two-programmer method A simpler estimation formula used in the two-programmer variant is: N̂ = n₁r / k In this variant, one programmer seeds S known errors, and a second programmer (tester) finds n₁ total errors without knowing which are seeded. r = S + N₀ as before, and k = n₁ − s. This method is easier to apply but makes the same equal-detection-probability assumption. Steps of error seeding Introduce errors: A known number S of artificial errors is seeded into the program by an experienced programmer who records their locations. Test: The program is tested by an independent tester who does not know which errors are seeded and which are inherent. Measure: The total errors found n₁ and the seeded errors found s are counted; inherent errors found k = n₁ − s. Estimate and improve: The total inherent error count N̂ is computed; if N̂ is too high, more testing or debugging is performed and the process may be repeated. Error seeding estimation — pseudocode error_seeding.pseudo // Step 1: Seed known errors into the program S = 20 // number of seeded errors injected seed_locations = INJECT_ERRORS(program, S) // Step 2: Independent tester runs tests (blind to seed locations) test_results = RUN_TESTS(program) n1 = COUNT(test_results.errors_found) // total errors found // Step 3: Identify how many seeded errors were detected s = COUNT(test_results.errors_found WHERE error IN seed_locations) k = n1 - s // inherent errors detected // Step 4: Estimate total inherent errors (hypergeometric method) // N0 = n1 * (r - k) / k - 1, where r = S + N0 // Solve iteratively or use two-programmer shortcut: N_hat = CEIL(n1 * (S + N0_estimate) / k) // Simplified two-programmer formula: N_hat_simple = n1 * (S + N0_estimate) / k // Step 5: Decision IF N_hat > acceptable_threshold THEN RECOMMEND more testing or debugging ELSE RECOMMEND sufficient fault removal for release END IF Drawbacks and limitations Cost: Seeding errors requires an experienced programmer's time to inject realistic faults, and a separate tester must be available — doubling personnel requirements. Equal detection assumption: Seeded errors must be equally likely to be detected as inherent errors — if seeded errors are too obvious or too subtle compared to real faults, the estimate is biased. Representativeness: Artificially seeded errors may not match the type, severity, or location distribution of real inherent errors. Tester awareness: If the tester suspects seeding is in use, they may change their testing strategy, invalidating the statistical assumptions. Small sample bias: When few errors are detected (small k), the estimate N̂ becomes highly unstable and unreliable. Exam question — Mills' Error Seeding Model Q: Explain Mills' error seeding technique. Write the estimation formulas. List the steps and drawbacks. Model answer points: Inject S known errors; test blindly; use capture-recapture to estimate inherent fault count. Hypergeometric: N₀ = n₁(r − k)/k − 1; N̂ = ⌈N₀⌉ + 1. Two-programmer method: N̂ = n₁r/k. Steps: introduce errors → test → measure (n₁, s, k) → estimate and improve. Drawbacks: cost (two personnel); seeded errors must match detection likelihood of inherent errors; representativeness; small sample bias. Variables: S (seeded), s (seeded found), n₁ (total found), k = n₁ − s (inherent found), N̂ (estimated inherent total). ← 11.7 PNZ Model Key takeaways →

excerpt: Topic 11 — Key takeaways Key takeaways — exam revision ← Topic 11 11.1 Software Reliability: Probability of failure-free operation for fixed time in fixed environment; human-design dependent; no physical wear; lifecycle: many initial faults → testing → useful life → upgrades add new faults. Reliability attributes: ROCOF (failure frequency), MTTF (avg time between failures — execution time only), M
full_text: Topic 11 — Key takeaways Key takeaways — exam revision ← Topic 11 11.1 Software Reliability: Probability of failure-free operation for fixed time in fixed environment; human-design dependent; no physical wear; lifecycle: many initial faults → testing → useful life → upgrades add new faults. Reliability attributes: ROCOF (failure frequency), MTTF (avg time between failures — execution time only), MTTR (repair time), MTBF = MTTF + MTTR, POFOD (failure on demand), availability, fault tolerance, recoverability, redundancy. Hardware vs software: Hardware = bathtub curve, wear, time-dependent; Software = no bathtub, no wear, redundancy can improve reliability, no preventive maintenance equivalent. High reliability strategies: Error avoidance (experienced devs, tools) → error detection (testing) → error removal (debugging) → fault tolerance (NVP, RB, rollback). 11.2 Fault Tolerance: Continue correct service despite faults; fault-removal (forward/backward recovery) vs fault-masking (NVP voting); design diversity. Basic techniques: Redundancy, checkpointing, error detection/correction, load balancing, isolation, replication, dynamic reconfiguration. Recovery Block (RB): Sequential — primary P, acceptance test T, alternates Q1..Qn; `Ensure T By P Else by Q1...`; rollback state on failure; better for non-real-time. N-Version Programming (NVP): N independent versions run in parallel; voter selects result; no re-execution delay; controversial (common-mode failures). Checkpoint/Rollback: Save system state periodically; restore on failure — backward error recovery. 11.3 SRGMs: Mathematical models predicting reliability improvement during testing; black-box; guide testing and resource allocation; Type-I (failure-count) vs Type-II (time-between-failures) data. 11.4 Jelinski-Moranda (1972): Binomial type; N initial faults; perfect debugging; λ(ti) = φ[N-(i-1)]; R(ti) = e^(-φ[N-(i-1)]ti); tends to be optimistic. 11.5 Schick-Wolverton (1978): JM modification; failure rate increases with time since last failure: λ(ti) = φ[N-(i-1)]ti; R(ti) = e^(-φ[N-(i-1)]ti²/2). 11.6 Goel-Okumoto (1979): Exponential NHPP; m(t) = a(1-e^(-bt)); a = total faults, b = detection rate; perfect debugging; widely used. 11.7 PNZ Model: NHPP with imperfect debugging; S-shaped detection rate; faults introduced during debugging (rate α); a(t)=a(1+αt); evaluates fault tolerance benefit. 11.8 Mills Error Seeding: Inject n₁ known errors; find k seeded in r removed; estimate N̂ = [n₁(r-k)/k - 1]+1; alternative: two programmers N̂ = n₁r/k; hypergeometric distribution. Model comparison: JM/SW = time-between-failure; GO/PNZ = NHPP mean value; Mills = fault count estimation (not growth curve). ← 11.8 Mills' Error Seeding Model Topic 12 →

title: 12.1 Reusability
headings: Reusability vs re-engineering vs reverse engineering
excerpt: Topic 12 — 12.1 Reusability 12.1 Reusability ← Topic 12 Software reusability is the practice of using existing software artifacts — components, modules, libraries, frameworks, and design knowledge — to build new systems rather than developing everything from scratch. Reusability is a core advanced issue in the course outline and directly addresses the software crisis — the gap between what hardwar
full_text: Topic 12 — 12.1 Reusability 12.1 Reusability ← Topic 12 Software reusability is the practice of using existing software artifacts — components, modules, libraries, frameworks, and design knowledge — to build new systems rather than developing everything from scratch. Reusability is a core advanced issue in the course outline and directly addresses the software crisis — the gap between what hardware can deliver and what software can produce — described in §1.6 Software Crisis. What is software reuse? Software reuse is the process of creating software systems from existing software systems and knowledge, rather than building every system from scratch. Reuse applies existing software artifacts — code, designs, specifications, test cases, and documentation — to reduce development effort on new projects. Reusability is listed as a quality attribute in McCall's model (product transition factor) — see §8.5 Quality Frameworks — and as an SQA focus area in §8.2 SQA. Reuse is an umbrella activity in software engineering — it runs parallel to the SDLC alongside SCM, SQA, and technical reviews. Why reusability matters Reusability reduces development time and cost by avoiding repeated implementation of already-solved problems. Reused components are typically better tested and more reliable because they have been exercised in prior projects. Reuse improves consistency across products — the same authentication module or logging library behaves identically everywhere it is used. It helps organisations respond faster to new requirements by assembling proven building blocks rather than coding from zero. Without deliberate reuse strategies, every project reinvents utilities, interfaces, and algorithms — multiplying maintenance cost and defect risk. Types of software reuse Code reuse: Reusing source code modules, functions, or libraries — e.g. importing a math library or copying a validated sorting routine into a new project. Design reuse: Reusing architectural or detailed design solutions — e.g. applying a layered architecture or a proven module decomposition from a prior system. Specification reuse: Reusing requirements or interface specifications — e.g. adopting a standard API specification or a reusable requirements template for a common domain. Component reuse: Reusing self-contained, independently deployable software components with well-defined interfaces — the foundation of component-based development. Framework reuse: Reusing an application skeleton that defines the overall structure — developers fill in domain-specific code within the framework's predefined hooks. Pattern reuse: Reusing proven design-level solutions to recurring problems — design patterns are a form of design reuse; see §12.2 Design Patterns. Characteristics of reusable components Generic and domain-independent where possible: A reusable component solves a general problem (e.g. date formatting) rather than one project's specific case. Modular and loosely coupled: The component has a well-defined interface and minimal dependencies on other modules — high cohesion, low coupling — see §5.10 Coupling and Cohesion. Well-documented: Clear documentation of purpose, interface, preconditions, and usage examples is essential for others to adopt the component. Thoroughly tested: Reusable components must be validated independently so that adopting projects inherit reliability rather than hidden defects. Version-controlled: Components must be tracked in a repository with version numbers so consuming projects know exactly which version they depend on. Configurable: Parameters or configuration files allow the same component to adapt to different contexts without modifying its source code. DRY principle — Don't Repeat Yourself The DRY principle states that every piece of knowledge in a system should have a single, authoritative representation — duplication of logic should be avoided. DRY promotes code reuse by centralising shared logic in one place, so changes need to be made only once rather than in multiple locations. Centralised logic reduces errors, simplifies maintenance, and ensures consistent behaviour across the codebase. DRY works with modular design and the Single Responsibility Principle to build scalable, maintainable systems. dry_principle.pseudo // NON-DRY — validation logic duplicated in multiple functions FUNCTION registerUser(email) IF NOT contains("@") OR length(email) < 5 THEN RETURN error ... END FUNCTION FUNCTION updateProfile(email) IF NOT contains("@") OR length(email) < 5 THEN RETURN error // duplicated! ... END FUNCTION // DRY — single reusable validation function FUNCTION isValidEmail(email) RETURN contains("@") AND length(email) >= 5 END FUNCTION FUNCTION registerUser(email) IF NOT isValidEmail(email) THEN RETURN error ... END FUNCTION FUNCTION updateProfile(email) IF NOT isValidEmail(email) THEN RETURN error // reuses same logic ... END FUNCTION Component-based architecture Component-Based Architecture — software is assembled from reusable components, each encapsulating specific functionality with well-defined interfaces. Component-Based Architecture (CBA) organises software into reusable parts, each serving a specific function with well-defined interfaces. Components encapsulate both data and behaviour — they interact through interfaces without exposing internal implementation details. Components can be developed, tested, and deployed independently, then combined to form complete applications. CBA promotes modularity, flexibility, and reusability — the same login component can be reused across web, mobile, and desktop applications. Library modules in function-oriented design are an early form of component reuse — see §5.14 Function-Oriented Design. Reuse-Oriented Model (ROM) The Reuse-Oriented Model (also called Reuse-Oriented Development) builds new systems primarily by identifying, modifying, and integrating components from existing systems rather than writing new code from scratch. Identify reusable components: Examine the old system and select components most suitable for reuse in the new system. Understand all components: Analyse the selected components to understand their interfaces, dependencies, and behaviour fully. Modify components: Adapt old components to meet new requirements — this may involve interface changes, performance tuning, or feature additions. Integrate into new system: Combine all modified (and any newly created) parts into the complete new system. Reuse can begin at any lifecycle phase — requirements, planning, design, code, or test data — not only during coding. ROM reduces total development cost, lowers risk, saves time, and is efficient when a sufficient library of reusable components exists. ROM requires a catalogue or framework for categorising components and tracking required modifications. If the reusable component library is insufficient, ROM may force compromises in requirements, producing a system that does not fully meet user needs. Incompatible old components can impact system evolution if their interfaces do not align with new technology choices. reuse_oriented_model.pseudo REUSE_ORIENTED_DEVELOPMENT(new_system, existing_systems[]) STEP 1 — IDENTIFY FOR each existing_system DO SCAN modules, libraries, interfaces SELECT candidates matching new_system requirements RECORD in reuse catalogue with compatibility notes END FOR STEP 2 — UNDERSTAND FOR each selected component DO ANALYSE interface, dependencies, behaviour, constraints DOCUMENT findings and modification needs END FOR STEP 3 — MODIFY FOR each component requiring change DO ADAPT interface / logic to meet new requirements TEST modified component independently VERSION and publish to component repository END FOR STEP 4 — INTEGRATE ASSEMBLE modified + new components into new_system RUN integration and system tests VERIFY new_system meets all requirements END REUSE_ORIENTED_DEVELOPMENT Reuse Maturity Model The Reuse Maturity Model describes how organisations evolve their reuse practices from

title: 12.2 Design Patterns
excerpt: Topic 12 — 12.2 Design Patterns 12.2 Design Patterns ← Topic 12 Design patterns are reusable, proven solutions to common problems encountered during the design and implementation of software systems. They were popularised by the Gang of Four (GoF) — Gamma, Helm, Johnson, and Vlissides — in their 1994 book Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software. Patterns represent design-lev
full_text: Topic 12 — 12.2 Design Patterns 12.2 Design Patterns ← Topic 12 Design patterns are reusable, proven solutions to common problems encountered during the design and implementation of software systems. They were popularised by the Gang of Four (GoF) — Gamma, Helm, Johnson, and Vlissides — in their 1994 book Design Patterns: Elements of Reusable Object-Oriented Software. Patterns represent design-level reuse — see §12.1 Reusability — and extend the OOD principles from §5.15 Object-Oriented Design. What is pattern designing? Pattern designing is the application of design patterns — reusable and proven solutions — to common problems during software design and implementation. Design patterns are general solutions to recurring design problems that have been tried and tested in real-world scenarios. They provide a set of proven approaches that promote best practices in software development. Patterns are programming language-independent strategies — they represent ideas, not particular implementations. Using patterns makes code more flexible, reusable, and maintainable when applied to the right problem. Patterns are not mandatory for every project — they are meant for common problem-solving, applied when a suitable recurring problem is identified. Design patterns provide reusable, proven solutions to recurring design problems in software systems. Pattern structure (GoF template) Every design pattern is documented using a standard template with four key elements: Pattern name: A meaningful name that captures the essence of the pattern — e.g. Singleton, Observer, Factory Method — establishing a common vocabulary for developers. Problem: The recurring design problem or situation where the pattern applies — when to use it and what forces or constraints exist. Solution: The proven design structure — the classes, objects, and relationships that resolve the problem. Consequences: The results of applying the pattern — trade-offs in flexibility, complexity, performance, and maintainability. Characteristics of pattern designing Problem-solution approach: Patterns identify common design problems and provide well-defined, proven solutions effective in similar contexts. Reusability: Patterns encapsulate successful design practices that can be applied across multiple projects, saving time and effort. Abstraction: Patterns abstract away specific implementation details and focus on high-level design concepts. Common vocabulary: Patterns establish standard terms that developers use to communicate design decisions — saying "Observer" conveys a full design intent. Proven solutions: Patterns are based on collective community experience, not arbitrary design choices — they represent solutions tested in real projects. Three categories of design patterns (GoF) The Gang of Four categorises 23 design patterns into three groups based on their purpose: CategoryPurposeKey ideaExample patterns CreationalObject creation mechanismsAbstract how objects are instantiatedSingleton, Factory Method, Abstract Factory, Builder, Prototype StructuralClass and object compositionCombine objects into larger structuresAdapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade, Flyweight, Proxy BehavioralObject communication and responsibilityDefine how objects interact and distribute workObserver, Strategy, Command, State, Template Method, Visitor 1. Creational design patterns Creational patterns abstract the instantiation process — they make a system independent of how its objects are created, composed, and represented. They encapsulate knowledge about which concrete classes the system uses and hide how instances are created and assembled. Two recurring themes: encapsulating which concrete class is used; hiding how instances are created. Singleton: Ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global access point — used for database connections, configuration managers, and logging systems. Factory Method: Defines an interface for creating objects but lets subclasses decide which class to instantiate — decouples object creation from the code that uses objects. Abstract Factory: Provides an interface for creating families of related objects without specifying their concrete classes. Builder: Separates the construction of a complex object from its representation — same construction process can create different representations. Prototype: Creates new objects by copying an existing object (prototype) rather than creating from scratch — useful when object creation is expensive. Singleton pattern — example The Singleton pattern ensures only one object of a class exists in the application. All clients receive the same shared instance — e.g. one DatabaseConnectionManager for the entire application. Prevents accidental creation of multiple instances and ensures efficient, consistent use of shared resources. Can use lazy initialization (created on first access) or eager initialization (created at class load time). singleton_pattern.pseudo CLASS DatabaseConnectionManager PRIVATE STATIC instance = NULL PRIVATE connectionString // Private constructor — prevents external instantiation PRIVATE CONSTRUCTOR DatabaseConnectionManager(connStr) connectionString = connStr END CONSTRUCTOR PUBLIC STATIC FUNCTION getInstance(connStr) IF instance IS NULL THEN instance = NEW DatabaseConnectionManager(connStr) END IF RETURN instance END FUNCTION PUBLIC FUNCTION executeQuery(sql) // use shared connection END FUNCTION END CLASS // All clients get the same instance db1 = DatabaseConnectionManager.getInstance("localhost:5432") db2 = DatabaseConnectionManager.getInstance("localhost:5432") // db1 IS db2 → only ONE instance exists Factory Method pattern — example The Factory Method defines an interface for creating objects but lets subclasses decide which concrete class to instantiate. The client code works with the abstract product interface — it does not need to know the concrete class being created. New product types can be added by creating new subclasses without modifying existing client code — supports the Open/Closed Principle. factory_method_pattern.pseudo ABSTRACT CLASS DocumentCreator ABSTRACT FUNCTION createDocument() // Factory Method FUNCTION processDocument() doc = createDocument() doc.open() doc.save() END FUNCTION END CLASS CLASS PDFCreator EXTENDS DocumentCreator FUNCTION createDocument() RETURN NEW PDFDocument() END FUNCTION END CLASS CLASS WordCreator EXTENDS DocumentCreator FUNCTION createDocument() RETURN NEW WordDocument() END FUNCTION END CLASS // Client uses abstract creator — no knowledge of concrete type creator = NEW PDFCreator() creator.processDocument() // creates and processes a PDF 2. Structural design patterns Seven structural design patterns — concerned with how classes and objects are composed into larger structures. Structural patterns are concerned with how classes and objects are composed to form larger structures while keeping those structures flexible and efficient. They help independently developed class libraries work together and allow new functionality through object composition at runtime. Adapter: Converts the interface of a class into another interface clients expect — lets incompatible classes work together (wrapper pattern). Bridge: Separates an abstraction from its implementation so both can vary independently — avoids permanent binding between abstraction and implementation. Composite: Composes objects into tree structures to represent part-whole hierarchies — clients treat individual objects and compositions uniformly. Decorator: Adds new responsibilities to objects dynamically without altering their structure — wraps the original object with additional functionality. Facade: Provides a unified, simplified interface to a set of interfaces in a subsystem — makes the subsystem easier to use. Flyweight: Shares common data across multiple objects to reduce memory usage — separates intrinsic (shared) state from extrinsic (unique) state. Proxy: Provides a surrogate or placeholder to control acces

title: Key takeaways — exam revision
excerpt: Topic 12 — Key takeaways Key takeaways — exam revision ← Topic 12 12.1 Reusability: Software reuse = building new systems from existing artifacts and knowledge rather than from scratch; addresses software crisis; umbrella activity parallel to SDLC. Benefits: Reduced development time/cost; better tested and more reliable components; consistency across products; faster response to new requirements.
full_text: Topic 12 — Key takeaways Key takeaways — exam revision ← Topic 12 12.1 Reusability: Software reuse = building new systems from existing artifacts and knowledge rather than from scratch; addresses software crisis; umbrella activity parallel to SDLC. Benefits: Reduced development time/cost; better tested and more reliable components; consistency across products; faster response to new requirements. Types of reuse: Code, design, specification, component, framework, pattern reuse — each operates at a different lifecycle level. Reusable component traits: Generic, modular, low coupling/high cohesion, well-documented, thoroughly tested, version-controlled, configurable. DRY principle: Every piece of knowledge has a single authoritative representation; avoids duplicated logic and inconsistent maintenance. Component-Based Architecture (CBA): Software assembled from reusable, independently deployable components with well-defined interfaces; promotes modularity and flexibility. Reuse-Oriented Model (ROM) — 4 steps: (1) Identify reusable components, (2) Understand all components, (3) Modify components, (4) Integrate into new system. Requires component catalogue; risk if library is insufficient. Reuse Maturity Model: L1 single-project source pool; L2 multi-project source copy (maintenance nightmare); L3 ad hoc binary reuse (utility in own project); L4 controlled binary reuse with version tracking (Reuse/Release Equivalence Principle). Reuse vs re-engineering vs reverse engineering: Reuse = use existing artifacts for new systems; Re-engineering = improve legacy system without changing core purpose; Reverse engineering = recover design/requirements from existing code (8 steps). 12.2 Design Patterns: Reusable proven solutions to recurring design problems; GoF 1994; language-independent; design-level reuse (not ready-made code). GoF pattern template: Name, problem, solution, consequences. Three categories: Creational (object creation — Singleton, Factory, Abstract Factory, Builder, Prototype); Structural (composition — Adapter, Bridge, Composite, Decorator, Facade, Flyweight, Proxy); Behavioral (communication — Observer, Strategy, Command, State, Template Method, etc.). Singleton: One instance only; global access point; private constructor; used for DB manager, config, logging. Factory Method: Interface for creating objects; subclasses decide concrete class; decouples creation from use; supports Open/Closed Principle. Observer: One-to-many dependency; subject notifies all observers on state change; publish-subscribe; loosely coupled. Strategy: Family of interchangeable algorithms encapsulated in strategy classes; selected/switched at runtime; eliminates complex if-else chains. Patterns vs code reuse: Patterns = design-level blueprint; code reuse = implementation-level modules; complement each other; overuse without need adds complexity. Links: Patterns extend OOD (coupling/cohesion, §5.10); documented with UML class diagrams (§5.16); patterns are design-level form of reusability (§12.1). ← 12.2 Design Patterns Software Engineering →

Overview of technical reporting Use of library and information gathering Administering questionnaires Reviewing gathered information Technical exposition Topical arrangement, exemplification, definition, classification and division Causal analysis and effective exposition Technical narration, description, and argumentation Persuasive strategy Organizing information — brainstorming and organizing material Construction of formal outline and outlining conventions Electronic communication and generating solutions Polishing style — paragraphs and sentence structure Clarity, length, order, and avoiding pomposity Document design — structure, preamble, summaries, and abstracts Table of contents, footnotes, glossaries, and cross-referencing Plagiarism, citation, bibliography, index, and appendices Typesetting systems Creating the professional report (mechanical and graphical elements) Proposals and progress reports Leaflets, brochures, and handbooks Magazine articles and research papers Feasibility reports and project reports Technical research reports Manuals, documentation, and thesis Electronic documents — linear versus hierarchical structure Reference Material Technical Report Writing (8th ed.) — Pauley & Riordan, Houghton Mifflin Company Effective Technical Communication — Ashraf Rizvi, Tata McGraw-Hill
